title: Charlie’s Personal R for Psychology Repository
text:
Overview
This is an ongoing collection of functions/workflows/code snippets to run analyses in R, as per psychological science conventions. The initial goal of this project was to provide a workflow for data cleaning for psychology researchers, as I was unable to find something similar when I was a PhD student. I’m now expanding it to be a living repository of all of the R code I collect over the years.
I learned how to do many of these things from other people’s generously developed guides, which I have cited and linked throughout.

title: Charlie’s Personal R for Psychology Repository
section: Do you have code to share?
text:
Do you have code to share?
📧 charlie.pittaway@flinders.edu.au
⭐ If you find new packages, or better processes, please let me know!
⭐ If you have R code, and you’re happy to share it, I will add it to the page.

If the code relates to published data, it would be handy if you sent through the analysis code along with the data (as you would upload to OSF) so that I can run executable code and show examples of the output alongside a link to the publication.
If the data are not yet published, the analysis code is sufficient, and I can update the page when the data are published. Thank you for contributing!

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.1 Setting up your R project
So, you’ve finished collecting data in Qualtrics. Now what?
Before you import anything, it’s worth spending five minutes setting up a proper R Project. This keeps your files organised and makes it much easier to upload a clean, self-contained folder to OSF later, since everything the project needs lives in one place with relative (not absolute) file paths.1

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
In RStudio: File → New Project → New Directory → New Project.
Give it a sensible name that represents the study or package of studies (e.g. Longitudinal analysis of temporal orientation) and choose where to save it.
Inside that project folder, create a few subfolders to keep things tidy. My standard folders are: data (where the raw and cleaned data files live), scripts (where data cleaning and analysis code lives), and outputs (figures, tables, and exported results).







ImportantImportant



Save your raw Qualtrics export into data/ as soon as you download it, and don’t edit it directly — treat it as read-only. Any cleaning should happen in a script, not in the file itself, so you (or anyone else) can always trace exactly what was done to the data and reproduce it.

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.2 Installing R packages
R has a completely bonkers amount of versatility. There is a custom built package for most things. To install a package, select Tools from the toolbar along the top of the window, then “Install packages” and then search for the package you are looking for. You can also install using code, as below. Once you have installed a package, you must load it before using it.
install.packages("package_name") #Load package
library(package_name) # Install package

title: 1  Getting Started
text:
1.3 Importing data into R
You’re probably familiar with downloading your data from Qualtrics in .sav format to import into SPSS, or .csv format to open in Excel. Both of these file types (and many more) can be used in R.
My recommendation is to save as an .sav file for the utility of having the item wording retained, which you lose in .csv format. To do this, you’ll need to install the haven package (Wickham et al., 2025).

install.packages("haven")
library(haven)

# Import your data into R, using the relative file path
dat <- read_sav("data/my_qualtrics_export.sav")

If you don’t care about retaining the item stem and .csv is sufficient, you’ll need the readr package instead (Wickham et al., 2026).

install.packages("readr")
library(readr)

# Import your data into R, using the relative file path
dat <- read_csv("data/my_qualtrics_export.csv")







WarningCommon error



If read_sav() throws an error about the file not being found, double check you’re working inside your .Rproj (look for the project name in the top-right corner of RStudio) and that the file path matches your actual folder structure, e.g. "data/filename.sav" not just "filename.sav".


Now that you have your data saved and your R project structure set up, you are ready to move on to data cleaning…




Wickham, H., Hester, J., & Bryan, J. (2026). Readr: Read rectangular text data. https://readr.tidyverse.org


Wickham, H., Miller, E., & Smith, D. (2025). Haven: Import and export ’SPSS’, ’stata’ and ’SAS’ files. https://haven.tidyverse.org

title: 1  Getting Started
text: An absolute path is the full file location starting from the root of your computer’s file system, e.g. "C:/Users/yourname/Documents/study1_analysis/data/my_data.sav" on Windows. This works fine on your machine, but breaks the moment the folder is moved, renamed, or opened by someone else. A relative path, like "data/my_data.sav", is defined relative to wherever the .Rproj file lives, so it keeps working no matter who opens the folder or where it’s saved.↩︎

text:
2.1 Loading full dataset from file
Using haven for .sav files (Wickham et al., 2025):
Using readr from tidyverse for .csv files (Wickham et al., 2019):
Using base R for R files.

title: 2  Selecting/Deselecting Variables
text:
library(haven)
data <- read_sav("data/my_qualtrics_export.sav")

library(tidyverse)
data <- readr::read_csv("data/my_qualtrics_export.csv")

title: 2  Selecting/Deselecting Variables
text:
2.2 Loading reduced dataset
Perhaps your data contains many variables that are not relevant to the current project, you can load your data with specific variables only.
You can use the select() function from tidyverse to specify which variables you would like.
data <- read_csv("data/my_data.csv") %>%
  dplyr::select(var1, var2, var3)






WarningCommon Error



I often find that R produces errors when I do not include dplyr:: before the select() function. I’m not really sure why this is – perhaps there is a conflict between two packages I often have loaded at the same time – but whatever the reason, my recommendation is to always make sure you’re specifying that you’re calling the select() function from the dplyr package in this way: dplyr::select()

title: 2  Selecting/Deselecting Variables
text:
2.3 Deleting specific variables
Perhaps you want most of the variables, but there are a select few that you would like to drop. You can use the tidyverse package to drop specific variables

2.3.1 When loading the data
data <- read_csv("data/my_data.csv") %>%
  dplyr::select(-var1, -var2, -var3)


2.3.2 In an already loaded dataframe
data <- data %>%
  dplyr::select(-var1)

title: 4  Recoding Variables
text:
4.1 Situation 1: Shifting a scale’s starting point
A collection of common recoding situations you’ll run into when preparing survey data for analysis.
Packages required:
In this first example, I have three items coded as 1–4, but I need them to be coded 0–3.
What this is doing:

title: 4  Recoding Variables
text:
# Recode variables from 1-4 to 0-3
data <- data %>%
  mutate(across(c(var1, var2, var3), ~ .x - 1, .names = "{.col}_r"))


across(c(var1, var2, var3),...) applies the same transformation to all three variables at once
~ .x - 1 is the transformation itself: .x stands in for “whatever variable is currently being processed,” and this subtracts 1 from every value in it.
.names = "{.col}_r" tells R to create new columns named var1_r, var2_r, and var3_r, rather than overwriting the original variables.

title: 4  Recoding Variables
text:
4.2 Situation 2: Reverse coding
In this second example, I have items coded 1–7 that need to be reverse-coded — i.e. a response of 1 should become 7, a response of 7 should become 1, and so on.
The formula for this is (n + 1) − original_value, where n is the highest point on the scale. For a 1–7 scale, that’s 8 − .x, because:

Original 1 becomes 8 − 1 = 7
Original 2 becomes 8 − 2 = 6
Original 3 becomes 8 − 3 = 5
Original 4 becomes 8 − 4 = 4 (midpoint stays the same)
Original 5 becomes 8 − 5 = 3
Original 6 becomes 8 − 6 = 2
Original 7 becomes 8 − 7 = 1

For multiple items at once:
data <- data %>%
  mutate(across(var1, var2, var3), ~ 8 - .x, .names = "{.col}_r"))
For a single item:
data <- data %>%
  mutate(var1_r = 8 - var1)
What this is doing:
This follows the same logic as the first example.

across(c(var1, var2, var3),...) applies the same transformation to all three variables at once
~ .x - 1 is the transformation itself: .x stands in for “whatever variable is currently being processed,” and this subtracts 1 from every value in it.
.names = "{.col}_r" tells R to create new columns named var1_r, var2_r, and var3_r, rather than overwriting the original variables.

The single item just skips across() since there’s only one variable to transform.
Adjusting the formula for other scale lengths:
To reverse-code for any scale, the formula is simply (highest scale point + 1) - original value.

title: 4  Recoding Variables
text:
4.3 Situation 3: Recoding factors/levels

4.3.1 Character variables to factors
Categorical variables are often stored as character (text) strings when they’re first imported — e.g. "control" and "treatment", or "low", "medium", "high". For most analyses, you’ll want these as a factor instead, since factors carry information about the category levels and their order, which R uses when running models or drawing legends.
Check the unique values first:
Before recoding, it’s worth checking exactly what values currently exist in the column — this catches typos, inconsistent capitalisation ("Control" vs "control"), or unexpected values before they cause problems downstream.
unique(data$condition)
What this is doing: unique() returns every distinct value present in data$condition, with duplicates removed — so you get a quick list of exactly what you’re working with.






TipTip



If you have a lot of variables to check, table(data$condition) is often more useful than unique() — it shows you the same distinct values, but also how many times each one appears, which makes typos and rare stray values (e.g. a single row with “contorl”) much easier to spot.



4.3.1.1 Converting to a factor — the default (alphabetical) behavior
If you convert a character variable straight to a factor without specifying anything else, R will order the levels alphabetically by default.
data <- data %>%
  mutate(condition = factor(condition))
What this is doing:

factor() converts the character column into a factor, but since no level order was specified, R falls back to sorting the distinct values alphabetically.
So "control" and "treatment" would end up ordered as control = 1, treatment = 2. This becomes a problem when you have something like "low", "medium", "high", which would end up being ordered high = 1, low = 2, medium = 3, which is probably not what you want.
This matters because the first level of a factor becomes the reference/baseline category in regression models by default. If R has chosen this alphabetically rather than by your intended design, your mode output and coefficient interpretation could be wrong.



4.3.1.2 Converting to a factor — with your own level order
To control the order (and therefore which level is treated as the reference/baseline), specify it explicitly using levels =:
data <- data %>%
  mutate(condition = factor(condition, levels = c("control", "treatment")))
What this is doing: the levels = c(...) argument tells R exactly which order to use, overriding the alphabetical default. Whatever value you list first becomes the reference level in models.
For a naturally ordered scale (like low/medium/high), the same approach applies:
data <- data %>%
  mutate(condition = factor(condition, levels = c("low", "medium", "high")))
If the variable has a genuine order (not just categories, but a rank — like low/medium/high), you can also mark it as an ordered factor using ordered = TRUE. This tells R the levels are not just labels, but have a true ranked order.
data <- data %>%
  mutate(condition = factor(condition, levels = c("low", "medium", "high"), ordered = TRUE))

title: 4  Recoding Variables
text:
4.4 Situation 4: Dummy coding
Perhaps you want to recode your descriptive or factor variable to a numeric variable, e.g., 0 and 1.
data <- data %>%
  mutate(new_variable = case_when(
    old_variable == "control" ~ 0,
    TRUE ~ 1
  ))
What this is doing:

case_when() checks each row against a series of conditions, in order, and assigns a value based on the first one that matches.
old_variable == "control" ~ 0 is the first condition: wherever old_variable equals "control", assign 0 to the new column.
TRUE ~ 1 acts as the catch-all/default: for any row that didn’t match the condition above, assign 1. Since TRUE is always true, this line always matches whatever’s left over — so in a two-group variable like control/treatment, everything that isn’t “control” (i.e. `“treatment”`) gets coded as 1.
The result, new_variable, is a plain numeric column (not a factor) — useful when a function or model specifically expects 0/1 numeric input rather than a labeled factor.


4.4.1 Using the FastDummies package
fastDummies (Kaplan, 2026) is a package which you can quickly dummy-code multiple variables at once. You could create dummy variables individually with case_when() (as above), but that means writing one line per level, per variable — a tedious and error-prone process once you have several categorical variables with multiple levels each. The dummy_cols() function in fastDummies does this for as many variables as you like, at any number of levels, in a single function call.
library(fastDummies)

data <- dummy_cols(data, select_columns = c("gender", "state", "age_bracket", "ethnicity"))
What this is doing:

dummy_cols() takes a dataframe and one or more categorical columns, and creates a new binary (0/1) column for every level found in each of those columns.
select_columns = c("gender", "state", "age_bracket", "ethnicity") tells it exactly which columns to dummy-code at once.
By default, dummy_cols() keeps the original columns and just adds the new dummy columns alongside them — so data ends up with both gender(original) and new columns like gender_male, gender_female (or gender_1, gender_2; one per level found in gender), plus the same pattern repeated for the other four variables.

There’s also a function to drop the first level of a variable, if necessary:
data <- dummy_cols(data, select_columns = c("gender", "state", "age_bracket", "ethnicity", remove_first_dummy = TRUE))

Turn this feature on if you’re using dummy-coded variables in regression models, as including a dummy column for every level of a variable, alongside an intercept, creates perfect multicollinearity (known as the “dummy variable trap”). Dropping one level resolves this: the dropped level becomes your implicit reference/baseline category, and every other dummy column’s coefficient is interpreted relative to it.
Levels are dropped alphabetically by default.

title: 4  Recoding Variables
text:
4.5 Situation 5: Effect coding
Effect coding (also called contrast coding) uses −1 and +1 instead of 0/1 to represent a two-level categorical variable. This changes how the coefficient in a regression model is interpreted: with effect coding, the coefficient represents the deviation from the grand mean, rather than the difference from a reference group (as with standard 0/1 dummy coding; (Cohen et al., 2013).
The same process is applied to create effect coded variables as above:
data <- data %>%
  mutate(condition_effect = case_when(
    condition == "control" ~ -1,
    condition == "treatment" ~ 1
  ))
The result, condition_effect, can be dropped straight into a regression model in place of the original grouping variable, giving you effect-coded coefficients.
When to use this:

When you’re running ANOVA-style designs through the lm() function. When you have a factorial design and want to run it as a regression rather than through aov(), effect coding makes the intercept represent the grand mean across all conditions, matching how ANOVA output is typically interpreted (Kugler et al., 2018).

With standard dummy coding (0/1), the intercept instead represents the mean of whichever group happens to be coded 0, which usually isn’t what you want to report.
Dummy coding and effect coding will yield the same omnibus F statistic, but can produce different estimates, test statistics, and p-values for main and interaction effects.

When you’re interpreting main effects in the presence of interactions. In models with interaction terms, effect-coded main effects can be interpreted as averaged across the levels of the other factor(s).

With dummy coding, the coefficient represents the effect only within the reference group, which changes the interpretation substantially once an interaction is in the model (Cohen et al., 2013).

When you’re comparing coefficient magnitudes across multiple categorical predictors, because effect-coded predictors are more directly comparable to one another than dummy coded coefficients would be.
However, if your research question is specifically about comparing every other group back to one meaningful reference group (e.g., every treatment conditions vs a single control condition), then standard 0/1 dummy coding is usually more interpretable.


4.5.1 Situation 5: Converting NAs to 0

4.5.1.1 For one variable
Using base R:
data$variable[is.na(data$variable)] <- 0
What this is doing:

is.na(data$variable) checks every value in the column and returns TRUE wherever it’s missing, FALSE otherwise.
data$variable[...] uses that TRUE/FALSE result to select only the missing entries.
<- 0 assigns 0 to just those selected (missing) entries, leaving every other value in the column untouched.



4.5.1.2 For multiple, specific variables
Using tidyverse syntax:
data <- data %>%
  mutate(across(c(var1, var2, var3), ~ replace_na(.x, 0)))
What this is doing:

across(c(var1, var2, var3), ...) applies the same transformation to each of the three named variables in turn, rather than writing a separate line for each one.
~ replace_na(.x, 0) is the transformation: .x stands in for whichever variable is currently being processed, and replace_na(.x, 0) replaces any NA in it with 0.
This overwrites var1, var2, and var3 in place. If you’d rather keep the originals and create new columns instead, add .names = "{.col}_r" as in earlier examples.



4.5.1.3 For the whole dataframe
Using tidyverse syntax:
data <- data %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ replace_na(.x, 0)))
What this is doing:

across(everything(), ...) applies the transformation to every column in the dataframe, rather than a specified subset.
~ replace_na(.x, 0) replaces any NA with 0, the same as above, just run across all columns instead of a chosen few.







ImportantImportant



Applying this to the whole dataframe will also affect any character or factor columns that happen to contain NA, not just numeric ones. replace_na(.x, 0) on a character column will throw an error (since 0 isn’t a valid character value), so this whole-dataframe version really only works cleanly if every column is numeric. If your dataframe has a mix of column types, it’s safer to either subset to just the numeric columns first (across(where(is.numeric), ...)) or stick with the named-variable version above.

title: 4  Recoding Variables
section: 4.6 Situation 6: Replacing a specific value with another
text:
4.6 Situation 6: Replacing a specific value with another

4.6.1 Special missing to NA
Sometimes a placeholder value has been used during data collection (e.g. `-99` for “not applicable” or “prefer not to say”) and needs to be converted to a proper NA before analysis.

4.6.1.1 For a single variable
Using base R:
data$variable[data$variable == -99] <- NA


4.6.1.2 For every column in the dataframe at once
Using tidyverse syntax:
data <- data %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ na_if(.x, -99)))
Or, if you’d rather use base R:
data[data == -99] <- NA



4.6.2 SPSS special missing
If special missing values have been used in SPSS (e.g., -99 or 999 tagged as missing, but different from NA), there is a function in the haven package to quickly convert them all to NA. I have not used this myself, but you can find out about this function here (Wickham et al., 2025).

text:
4.7 References

title: 5  Row/ID Numbers
text:
If you want to add a sequential numbered variable for the number of rows in the dataframe, such as an ID code, use the following code using base R:

data$id <1:nrow(data)

This will add a variable to your dataframe called ‘id’, which numbers your participants sequentially.

title: 6  Mean-Centring and Standardising
text:
6.1 Mean-Centring
Mean centering is the process of subtracting the mean of a variable from each of its values, effectively shifting the data so that the new mean is zero. Variables involved in interaction terms are often mean-centred before the interaction is calculated. This helps reduce the correlation between the interaction term and its component variables (i.e., reduces multicollinearity), which can improve the stability and interpretability of the regression coefficients (Iacobucci et al., 2015). Mean-centring does not change the distribution of a variable (i.e., the standard deviation remains the same), which means it does not change the overall fit of the model or the significance of the interaction.
This is particularly useful when interpreting interactions (Goldstein, 2023). In a regression model, the main effect of a predictor is interpreted as its effect when the interacting variable equals 0. Because a score of 0 is often impossible or not meaningful on psychological scales, these main effects can be difficult to interpret. After mean-centring, a value of 0 corresponds to the average score on the variable. As a result, the main effects can be interpreted as the effects of a predictor when the interacting variable is at its average level, and the intercept represents the predicted outcome for a person with average scores on all centred predictors.
There is no point to mean-centring a variable when:

title: 6  Mean-Centring and Standardising
text:
There is no interaction terms containing that variable
The variable has a meaningful 0 value


6.1.1 How to mean-centre variables in R
You can mean-centre variables using a base R function:
data$variable_mc <- scale(data$variable, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE)
Or, if you wanted to mean-centre multiple variables at once:
data <- data %>%
  mutate(variable_1_mc = scale(variable_1, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE),
         variable_2_mc = scale(variable_2, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE),
         variable_3_mc = scale(variable_3, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE))

title: 6  Mean-Centring and Standardising
text:
6.2 Standardising
Standardising a variable also centres a variable (sets the mean to 0), but it additionally sets the standard deviation to 1. This means that coefficients relating to standardised variables are now interpreted differently; a one-unit difference in the standardised variable equals a one-standard deviation difference.
Variables are standardised if there are many variables included in a model that are measured on different scales – standardisation allows the magnitudes of coefficients to be compared.

6.2.1 How to standardise variables in R
The same base R function is used to standardise as to mean centre.
data$variable_z <- scale(variable, center = FALSE, scale = TRUE) # One variable
data <- data %>%
  mutate(variable_1_z = scale(variable_1, center = FALSE, scale = TRUE),
         variable_2_z = scale(variable_2, center = FALSE, scale = TRUE))




Goldstein, N. (2023). Epidemiology blog of neal d. Goldstein, PhD, MBI. https://doi.org/10.17918/goldsteinepi


Iacobucci, D., Schneider, M. J., Popovich, D. L., & Bakamitsos, G. A. (2015). Mean centering helps alleviate “micro” but not “macro” multicollinearity. Behavior Research Methods, 48(4), 1308–1317. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13428-015-0624-x

title: 7  Deleting Rows and Columns
text:
7.1 Deleting rows
To remove a row in R, use the dplyr package from the tidyverse (Wickham et al., 2019) with the slice() function. Each row in R is assigned a number, so just subtract the row number that you want to delete.
For example, if you have imported a .csv file into R and you didn’t remove the first row of data (which is often whack when downloading from Qualtrics), you would use the following code to remove that row:

title: 7  Deleting Rows and Columns
text:
library(tidyverse) #Load dplyr package
data <- data %>%
slice(-1) #Minus the row number you're trying to remove

title: 7  Deleting Rows and Columns
text:
7.2 Deleting columns
To remove columns (i.e., variables) from a data frame, use the select() function with a minus sign before each variable to remove.
data <- data %>%   dplyr::select(-var1)






TipError Message



I can never run select() without also specifying that I’m pulling this function from the dplyr package. I’m not sure why this is, but now my general practice is to always specify dplyr::select().






Wickham, H., Averick, M., Bryan, J., Chang, W., McGowan, L. D., François, R., Grolemund, G., Hayes, A., Henry, L., Hester, J., Kuhn, M., Pedersen, T. L., Miller, E., Bache, S. M., Müller, K., Ooms, J., Robinson, D., Seidel, D. P., Spinu, V., … Yutani, H. (2019). Welcome to the tidyverse. Journal of Open Source Software, 4(43), 1686. https://doi.org/10.21105/joss.01686

title: 8  Appending Data
text:
8.1 Combining data frames
For whatever reason, you might have ended up with multiple separate data frames that need to be combined.
Using the dplyr package from the tidyverse (Wickham et al., 2019), append using the function bindrows().
This code will:
You can also add some specifications that might be helpful; for example, you can order by date and flag second+ occurrences of a particular participant ID.
Why might this be helpful?

title: 8  Appending Data
text:
data_master <- bind_rows(dataset_1, dataset_2, dataset_3) 


Keep all variables from all datasets
Fill in NA for variables that don’t exist in a particular version
Stack the rows on top of each other


data_full_t1 <- bind_rows(data_t1_v1, data_t1_v2, data_t1_v3, data_t1_v4) %>% 
  dplyr::select(-Q_TotalDuration) %>% mutate(ui_duplicate = duplicated(ui))


During a soft launch of a survey, we found that the survey was far too long and needed to create a shorter version. However, creating a shorter version meant substantially changing the structure of the survey, so we needed to create a whole new Qualtrics questionnaire rather than amending the active one.
This resulted in separate data frames that we needed to combine, but we also wanted to make sure that we only included the first instance of every participant ID, as launching a second survey meant that Qualtrics’ in-built ballot stuffing prevention measures wouldn’t apply and participants could legitimately take our study twice.
In order to keep only the first case of every participant ID (called ui in our data), we needed to order the data by date and then flag any subsequent instances, which we did so with the variable ui_duplicate

title: 8  Appending Data
text:
8.2 Combining longitudinal data
First, and crucially, you must make sure there are no duplicates in the separate waves.
Second, make sure you have created a variable that indicates which waves participants have completed (e.g., w1_sample == 1 or 0, and so on). Third, add a timepoint suffix to the variables in each wave. Fourth, combine the data frames, linking by participant ID. You can do these three steps at the same time.
data_wide <- data_t1 %>%
  mutate(w1_sample = 1) %>%
  rename_with(~ paste0(.x, "_t1"), -ui) %>%
  full_join(
    data_t2 %>%
      mutate(w2_sample = 1) %>%
      rename_with(~ paste0(.x, "_t2"), -ui),
    by = "ui"
  ) %>%
  full_join(
    data_t3 %>%
      mutate(w3_sample = 1) %>%
      rename_with(~ paste0(.x, "_t3"), -ui),
    by = "ui"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    across(starts_with("w"), ~ replace_na(.x, 0))
  )
What this is doing:

Creates wave-completion indicators (w1_sample, w2_sample, w3_sample), where a value of 1 indicates that a participant completed that wave.



Adds timepoint suffixes to all variables (e.g., stress_t1, stress_t2, stress_t3) while leaving the participant ID (ui) unchanged.



Merges the datasets by participant ID using full_join(), retaining all participants regardless of whether they completed every wave.

M., Müller, K., Ooms, J., Robinson, D., Seidel, D. P., Spinu, V., … Yutani, H. (2019). Welcome to the tidyverse. Journal of Open Source Software, 4(43), 1686. https://doi.org/10.21105/joss.01686

9.3 Saving as an R data file

The benefits of saving an R data file:

It preserves the complete R object, including data types (factors, dates, logicals, integers, lists, etc.), attributes (labels, metadata, row names, factor levels), complex objects (like models, lists, tibbles, spatial objects, plots, etc.), no additional parsing is required because data are saved exactly as they are, and they’re usually quicker to read than .cvs or .sav files.

title: 10  Data Cleaning: Step-by-Step
text:
10.1 Step 1: Participant exclusions
Researchers have different processes for preparing their data for analysis, often which appears to be by convention. On this page, I will set out my process and reasoning—take what works, leave what doesn’t. Notably, I conduct my reliability analysis and scale creation during my data cleaning process, as this makes the most sense for me.
The first step I like to do is exclude participants based on my pre-registered criteria. One example is filtering out likely bot responses using Qualtrics’ built-in reCAPTCHA score.
Q_RecaptchaScore values < 0.5: this is one of Qualtrics’ built-in measures. Scores range from 0 to 1; scores less than 0.5 are likely a bot, whereas scores greater than or equal to 0.5 are likely to be human.
What this is doing:
You can also exclude participants using the following code template:
What this is doing:

title: 10  Data Cleaning: Step-by-Step
text:
library(tidyverse)
data <- data %>%
  filter(Q_RecaptchaScore >= 0.5)


filter() keeps only the rows that meet the condition you give it, and drops every row that doesn’t — here, that condition is Q_RecaptchaScore >= 0.5.
Q_RecaptchaScore >= 0.5 checks each row’s score and evaluates to TRUE for anyone at or above the 0.5 threshold, FALSE for anyone below it. Only the TRUE rows are kept in the resulting data.
The result overwrites data, so from this point onward in your script, every subsequent step is working with the filtered sample rather than the original raw import. If you wanted to save this into a new dataframe, you would run the code data_filtered <- data %>% filter(Q_RecaptchaScore >= 0.5)


data <- data %>%
  filter(participant_type != "exclude")


In this example, I have a character variable called participant_type in which I have already flagged participants I wanted to exclude. The operators != indicate “does not equal,” so we are not retaining participants coded “exclude.”

title: 10  Data Cleaning: Step-by-Step
section: 10.2 Step 2: Drop, rename, and check variable types
text:
10.2 Step 2: Drop, rename, and check variable types
Next, I like to clean up my datafile and drop any unnecessary variables, rename them, and ensure that no variables which should be numeric are being read as character variables.

title: 10  Data Cleaning: Step-by-Step
text:
10.3 Step 3: Basic item-level descriptives
I then like to make sure there are no glaringly obvious issues with the data; for example, I haven’t accidentally mapped two scale response options to the same number. In that case, I would go back into Qualtrics and recode that response and then redownload my data.
I use the describe() function from the psych package (William Revelle, 2026) to quickly investigate: do the min/max values make sense? Are special missing data codes present (e.g., -99)? I will recode if necessary.

title: 10  Data Cleaning: Step-by-Step
text:
10.4 Step 4: Reliability and scale creation
Scale by scale, I conduct reliability analysis and create my scales. I use functions from the psych package (William Revelle, 2026) for scales with 3 or more items, and spearman_brown() from the splithalfr package (Pronk, 2025) for scales with only 2 items.

10.4.1 Cronbach’s alpha

library(psych)
alpha(data[, c("identity_1", "identity_2", "identity_3")], na.rm = T)

What this is doing:

dat[, c("var1", "var2", "var3")] selects just the columns making up your scale.
alpha() computes Cronbach’s alpha across those items, along with the fuller diagnostic output: item-total correlations, “alpha if item dropped,” and both raw and standardised alpha.
na.rm = TRUE tells alpha() to exclude missing values when calculating, rather than letting a single NA in any item cause the whole calculation to fail. Internally, this uses pairwise deletion for the correlation matrix alpha() relies on — meaning each pair of items is correlated using whatever rows have data for both of them, not just the rows that are complete across every item in the scale.







NoteReading the output



The value you’ll usually want to report is raw_alpha, which is the standard Cronbach’s alpha. The std.alpha value (standardised alpha) is relevant if your items are on notably different scales. The “alpha if item dropped” section shows what raw_alpha would be if that specific row’s item were removed from the scale. This is the most practically useful part of the output for scale refinement; e.g., if the reliability is below the acceptable threshold, perhaps this is due to one pesky item which should be dropped from the scale.




10.4.2 McDonald’s omega
Another measure of internal consistency is McDonald’s omega. Omega estimates the reliability of a scale using a factor-analytic model rather than a simple summed score (as with alpha).
Cronbach’s alpha assumes that all the items measure the underlying construct equally well, but in practice this assumption is rarely met—some items just measure a construct better than others! When this assumption is violated, Cronbach’s alpha tends to underestimate the scale reliability.
McDonald’s omega, on the other hand, run a factor analysis on the items first, then calculates reliability based on actual factor loadings so that items which load more strongly onto the factor are weighted more heavily than weaker items.

omega(data[, c("efficacy_1", "efficacy_2", "efficacy_3", "efficacy_4")], na.rm = T)

The output includes several different reliability coefficients, but the two most commonly reported are:

omega_h (omega hierarchical): this is the proportion of variance in total scores attributable to a single general factor underlying all items. This is the closest omega equivalent to “how much of this scale is measuring one coherent thing.”

This is typically what you want to report if using omega.

omega_t (omega total): this is the reliability of the total score treating all common variance (general factor plus any group/sub-factors) as true score. This is generally closer to what alpha() is estimating, but calculated via the factor model rather than raw covariances.

To my understanding, omega looks like it will be the way forward for new scales/psychometric development, or at least that it will be reported alongside alpha. However, it’s not really useful for short scales, in which case alpha (if 3 items) or the Spearman Brown coefficient (for 2 item scales) are more appropriate.


10.4.3 Spearman Brown Coefficient
Eisinga et al. (2013) compared Pearson r, Cronbach’s alpha, and the Spearman-Brown coefficient for assessing the reliability of two-item scales. They concluded that Pearson r is not appropriate (in fact, were quite sassy about it: they said “one may rather call that the reliability of a one-item test,” p. 641), and nor is Cronbach’s alpha. Instead, they recommended using the Spearman-Brown coefficient, which is generally higher than alpha and less biased on average.
You can calculate the Spearman-Brown coefficient using the splithalfr package (Pronk, 2025).

library(splithalfr)
spearman_brown(data$trust_1, data$trust_2,
               use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

[1] 0.8391863


What this is doing:

use = "pairwise.complete.obs" is necessary if you have any missing data; without this, if missing data is present, the result will be NA.



10.4.4 Scale creation
If reliability is sufficient, I create my scales using the tidyverse package (Wickham et al., 2019).
library(tidyverse)
data <- data %>%
  mutate(scale = rowMeans(across(c(var1, var2, var3)), na.rm = TRUE),
         scale = ifelse(is.nan(scale), NA, scale))
What’s happening here:

The first line creates scale and assigns it as a new column in dat.

across(c(var1, var2, var3)) selects the three items that make up the scale.
rowMeans(..., na.rm = TRUE) calculates the average of those three columns per row (i.e. per participant), rather than a single average across the whole column. na.rm = TRUE tells it to ignore any missing values when averaging, so a participant who answered 2 out of 3 items still gets a scale score based on those 2, rather than automatically becoming NA.

The second line, scale = ifelse(is.nan(scale), NA, scale), is a cleanup step: rowMeans() with na.rm = TRUE has a quirk where a row with no valid responses at all (all three items missing) doesn’t return NA like you’d expect — it returns NaN (“Not a Number”, R’s result for an undefined calculation like averaging zero values). This line checks for that specific case with is.nan() and converts it to a proper NA, so downstream functions (which generally know how to handle NA, but may not handle NaN the same way) treat it correctly as missing.



10.4.5 Alternatively… reliability and scale creation in one step
I have recently discovered that the psych package has a function called scoreItems() which allows you to calculate reliability and create multiple scales in one step. First, you define the variables that make up each scale and prefix any reverse-scored items with a minus sign. Next, you score all scales at once. Finally, you can print the reliability and descriptives for every scale at the same time.

# Define each scale's items, prefixing reverse-scored items with a minus sign
scale_keys <- list(
  trust = c("trust_1", "trust_2"), 
  identity = c("identity_1", "identity_2", "identity_3"), 
  efficacy = c("efficacy_1", "efficacy_2", "efficacy_3", "efficacy_4"),
  action = c("action_1", "action_2", "action_3", "action_4", "-action_5") # One reverse-coded item
  
)

# Score all scales at once
scores <- scoreItems(scale_keys, data)

# Check reliability and descriptives for every scale together
summary(scores)

Call: scoreItems(keys = scale_keys, items = data)

Scale intercorrelations corrected for attenuation 
 raw correlations below the diagonal, (unstandardized) alpha on the diagonal 
 corrected correlations above the diagonal:
          trust identity efficacy  action
trust     0.812   0.0930    0.128 -0.0189
identity  0.076   0.8234   -0.020  0.0081
efficacy  0.107  -0.0168    0.863  0.0327
action   -0.016   0.0069    0.028  0.8730

# Attach the scale scores back onto your main dataframe
data <- cbind(data, scores$scores)

What this is doing:

scale_keys is a named list, where each element defines one scale. The name you give each element (scale_1, scale_2, scale_3) becomes that scale’s column name in the output.
Within each scale’s item vector, a - prefix (e.g. "-var3") tells scoreItems() to automatically reverse-code that specific item before combining it with the rest — no need to manually create _r columns beforehand.
scoreItems(scale_keys, data) scores every scale defined in scale_keys in one call.
summary(scores) prints reliability (alpha) along the diagonal and basic descriptives for all three scales at once.
scores$scores is a matrix with one column per scale, each row matching the corresponding row in data. cbind(dat, scores$scores) binds these new columns onto your existing dataframe, so all three scale scores become permanent columns in data, ready to use in later analysis.







NoteNote



It goes without saying, but if using this approach, don’t reverse-code your items manually before hand, otherwise they will be double reverse-coded and just end up forward-coded again.

title: 10  Data Cleaning: Step-by-Step
text:
10.5 Step 6: Univariate Outliers
Some researchers like to transform univariate outliers, and some do not. Both options introduce different forms of bias (transforming that distort the underlying distribution and relationships between variables, while leaving outliers untouched can influence means, correlations, and model estimates). If you are the kind of researcher who would like to transform your univariate outliers, this is the approach that worked for me in the past.






NoteNote



Sometimes you might want to check for univariate outliers on individual items (e.g., for factor analysis, where the assumption is that outliers are not present); but generally, I would conduct this analysis on the scales rather than the items.



10.5.1 Identify scales with outliers
First, I developed a function to identify scales on which outliers were present, with outliers defined as those more than 3 standard deviations below or above the mean. In the running this function on our simulated data shows that outliers are present on the trust, identity, and efficacy scales.

detect_outliers <- function(df) {

  outlier_flags <- sapply(df, function(x) {

    if (is.numeric(x)) {

      any(
        x > (mean(x, na.rm = TRUE) + 3 * sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)) |
          x < (mean(x, na.rm = TRUE) - 3 * sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)),
        na.rm = TRUE
      )

    } else {

      FALSE

    }

  })

  outlier_results <- tibble(
    Variable = names(outlier_flags),
    Has_Outliers = outlier_flags
  )

  return(outlier_results)

}

# Run univariate outlier analysis
detect_outliers(data[, c("trust", "identity", "efficacy", "action")])

# A tibble: 4 × 2
  Variable Has_Outliers
  <chr>    <lgl>       
1 trust    TRUE        
2 identity TRUE        
3 efficacy TRUE        
4 action   FALSE       


What this is doing:

detect_outliers <- function(df) { ... } defines a custom function — rather than checking each variable one at a time, this packages the whole outlier-detection process so it can be reused on any set of columns, in this workbook or future projects, just by calling detect_outliers() on a new dataframe.
sapply(df, function(x) { ... }) runs the inner function on every column in df in turn, with x standing in for whichever column is currently being processed.
if (is.numeric(x)) { ... } else { FALSE } — this ensures that the function is only run on numeric variables. Any character, factor, or other non-numeric column just gets flagged FALSE (no outliers checked) automatically.
Inside the numeric branch, the outlier rule is: x > (mean + 3*sd) | x < (mean - 3*sd). This is the standard “3 SDs from the mean” definition of a univariate outlier — anything further than 3 standard deviations above or below the mean (the | means “or”) is flagged. mean(x, na.rm = TRUE) and sd(x, na.rm = TRUE) both ignore missing values when calculating, so a few NAs in a column don’t distort the threshold.
any(..., na.rm = TRUE) collapses that row-by-row TRUE/FALSE result down to a single answer per column: TRUE if any value in that column meets the outlier criteria, FALSE if none do. The na.rm = TRUE here (on any(), not the inner mean/sd calls) makes sure that a missing value in the comparison itself doesn’t cause the whole any() check to return NA instead of a real TRUE/FALSE.
tibble(Variable = names(outlier_flags), Has_Outliers = outlier_flags) takes the results — a named vector, one TRUE/FALSE per column — and turns it into a tidy two-column table: which variable, and whether it has any outliers.
df[, c("var1", "var2", "var3", "var4")] selects just the specific columns you want to check (your scale variables, say), rather than running the function on your entire dataframe.

The result is a small table listing every variable you checked and a TRUE/FALSE for whether it contains at least one outlier by this definition.


10.5.2 Winsorise outliers
If outliers are present, one technique is to Winsorise the items so that responses that are greater than three standard deviations above or below the mean are replaced with exactly 3 standard deviations above or below the mean. Again, I created a function to do this across multiple variables at once. The code below creates a new variable identifiable by a suffix ‘win’.

winsorise_outliers <- function(data, var_name, na.rm = TRUE) {
  # Get mean
  var_m <- mean(data[[var_name]], na.rm = na.rm)
  # Get sd
  var_sd <- sd(data[[var_name]], na.rm = na.rm)
  # Get lower and upper thresholds
  var_lower <- var_m - 3 * var_sd
  var_upper <- var_m + 3 * var_sd
  
  # Create new variable name with _win suffix
  new_var_name <- paste0(var_name, "_win")
  
  # Create new variable with suffix _win, replacing values beyond the thresholds with the threshold
  data[[new_var_name]] <- case_when(
    data[[var_name]] > var_upper ~ var_upper,
    data[[var_name]] < var_lower ~ var_lower,
    TRUE ~ data[[var_name]]
  )
  
  return(data)
}

data <- winsorise_outliers(data, "trust") # For one variable

To winsorise several outliers at once, create a list of variables and then loop over.

# Create list of variables to loop over
vars_to_winsorise <- c("trust", "identity", "efficacy")

# Run the loop
for(var in vars_to_winsorise) {
  data <- winsorise_outliers(data, var)
}

title: 10  Data Cleaning: Step-by-Step
text:
10.6 Step 7: Normality
Next, check for normality. If you have Winsorised scales with outliers, make sure to check the normality of the Winsorised scales rather than the raw scales.






NoteWhy outliers before normality?



Outliers inflate skewness and kurtosis statistics. Most normality checks are sensitive to extreme values. A handful of outliers can make an otherwise normal-ish distribution look meaningfully skewed. If you check normality first, you might conclude a variable is non-normal and needs transforming, when really it’s a few outliers doing the damage.



10.6.1 Skew and kurtosis
Skew measures asymmetry — whether one tail of the distribution is longer than the other.

A skew of 0 indicates a perfectly symmetric distribution.
Positive skew (a long tail stretching to the right) means most scores cluster at the lower end, with a few high outlying scores pulling the tail out.
Negative skew (a long tail stretching to the left) means most scores cluster at the higher end, with a few low outlying scores — common in ceiling-effect items.

Kurtosis measures “tailedness” — how heavy or light a distribution’s tails are relative to a normal distribution.

A kurtosis of 0 (using the excess kurtosis convention, which is what psych::describe() reports) indicates tails similar to a normal distribution.
Positive kurtosis (leptokurtic) means heavier tails and a sharper peak than normal — more extreme values than you’d expect under normality.
Negative kurtosis (platykurtic) means lighter tails and a flatter peak — fewer extreme values than expected, a more “spread out,” uniform-looking distribution.

Use the psych package (William Revelle, 2026) to check the skew and kurtosis. If you have Winsorised scales with outliers, make sure you use the Winsorised scales rather than the raw ones.

library(psych) 
describe(data[, c("trust_win", "identity_win", "efficacy_win", "action")]) 

             vars   n mean   sd median trimmed  mad  min  max range  skew
trust_win       1 500 4.01 1.00      4    4.00 0.74 1.00 7.00  6.00  0.04
identity_win    2 500 3.95 0.90      4    3.95 0.99 1.33 6.65  5.32 -0.03
efficacy_win    3 500 4.01 0.90      4    4.03 0.74 1.30 6.50  5.20 -0.21
action          4 500 4.03 0.97      4    4.04 0.89 1.20 6.80  5.60  0.00
             kurtosis   se
trust_win        0.08 0.04
identity_win     0.11 0.04
efficacy_win    -0.05 0.04
action          -0.31 0.04


What this is doing:

describe() produces a table of descriptive statistics for each variable — including mean, sd, min, max, and, importantly here, skew and kurtosis columns.

Interpreting the output:
There’s no single universally agreed cutoff, but a commonly used rule of thumb is that skew or kurtosis values beyond ±2 suggest a meaningful departure from normality.


10.6.2 Formal test of normality
The Shapiro-Wilk test gives you a formal statistical test of whether one variable’s distribution differs significantly from normal.

shapiro.test(data$trust_1)


    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  data$trust_1
W = 0.93836, p-value = 4.023e-13


The output gives you a test statistic (W) and a p-value. If significant, you reject the null hypothesis (i.e. your data significantly deviates from normal).






CautionSensitive to sample size



Shapiro-Wilk is notoriously sensitive to sample size [Yazici & Yolacan (2007)](Razali & Wah, 2011). With a large sample (roughly N > 300–500), even tiny, practically trivial deviations from normality will return a significant p-value.




10.6.3 Visual checks

10.6.3.1 Histogram/Density plot
A histogram gives you an intuitive sense of a variable’s shape, and is often more informative than a single test statistic, since you can actually see where any skew or unusual clustering is coming from.
You can do this really quickly using base R:

hist(data$action)








Or using ggplot2 from tidyverse (Wickham et al., 2019). The code below shows a normal curve on the graph – this means it is actually a density plot (rather than a histogram).

library(ggplot2)


Attaching package: 'ggplot2'


The following objects are masked from 'package:psych':

    %+%, alpha

#| label: Density plot with ggplot2
ggplot(data, aes(action)) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), colour = "black", fill = "skyblue", binwidth = 1) +
  labs(x = "Item Responses", y = "Density") +
  stat_function(fun = dnorm, args = list(mean = mean(data$action, na.rm = TRUE), sd = sd(data$action, na.rm = TRUE)), colour = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 7, 1)) + # First number = min scale, second number = max scale, last number leave as 1
  theme(text = element_text(size = 25))








What this is doing:

ggplot(dat, aes(var1)) sets up the plot, telling ggplot which dataframe and variable to use.
theme(legend.position = "none") removes the plot legend. Since this plot only has one histogram and one line (no grouping variable), there’s nothing a legend would usefully explain.
geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), ...) draws the histogram.

Normally a histogram’s y-axis shows raw counts (how many participants fell in each bin), but that can’t be directly compared to a normal curve, which is a probability density, not a count. In this code, aes(y = after_stat(density)). after_stat(density) tells ggplot to rescale the histogram bars so the total area under them sums to 1 — the same scale a density curve uses — so that a normal curve can be overlaid.

colour = "black" outlines each bar, fill = "skyblue" colors them in, and binwidth = 1 sets each bar to span 1 unit (sensible for a Likert-type item).
labs(x = "Item Responses", y = "Density") sets axis labels.
stat_function(fun = dnorm, args = list(mean = ..., sd = ...), ...) draws the actual normal curve.
theme_classic() applies a clean, minimal ggplot theme (removes the default grey background and gridlines).
scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 7, 1)) controls where the tick marks appear on the x-axis. seq(1, 7, 1) generates the sequence 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 — matching a 1–7 response scale, so every scale point gets its own labeled tick mark rather than R choosing arbitrary breaks. If your scale was scored differently, you’d adjust the first two numbers to match your actual scale’s minimum and maximum (e.g. seq(1, 5, 1) for a 1–5 scale).



10.6.3.2 Q-Q plot
A Q-Q (“quantile-quantile”) plot compares the data’s distribution directly against a theoretical normal distribution — points falling along the diagonal reference line indicate normality; systematic deviation from that line indicates non-normality, and the shape of the deviation tells you what kind.

ggplot(data, aes(sample = action)) +
  stat_qq() +
  stat_qq_line(color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Q-Q Plot of Variable 1")








What this is doing:

stat_qq() plots the data’s quantiles against the quantiles you’d expect if the data were perfectly normally distributed.
stat_qq_line() draws the reference diagonal line representing “perfect normality” — the closer your points hug this line, the closer your data is to normal.
Points curving away from the line at the ends (“tails”) typically indicate skew or heavy/light tails — the same issue skew and kurtosis values are picking up numerically, but here you can see exactly where in the distribution (upper end, lower end, or both) the deviation is occurring.




10.6.4 So what…?
None of these three checks — skew/kurtosis, Shapiro-Wilk, and visual plots — is sufficient on its own. A sensible workflow is: use skew/kurtosis as a fast first-pass screen across many variables at once (as in the previous section), then for any variable that looks borderline or concerning, follow up with a Shapiro-Wilk test and a histogram/Q-Q plot together, since the visual check will often explain why the formal test came back significant (e.g. “ah, it’s these three outlying values,” rather than a genuinely non-normal underlying shape).


10.6.5 Transforming skewed data
If you have determined that your data is skewed and you wish to transform it, here is some helpful code.
Which transformation to use depends on two things: how severe the skew is, and which direction it goes (positive or negative). For more detail on choosing between these approaches, see rcompanion’s handbook on transforming skewed data and Datanovia’s guide to normal distribution transformations.
As a general starting point: try a log transformation first if your DV increases more rapidly as your IV increases. Try a square transformation instead if your DV decreases more rapidly as your IV increases. The three options below (square root, log, inverse) are ordered from mildest to strongest — start with the mildest transformation that resolves the skew, rather than jumping straight to the strongest one available.

10.6.5.1 Square root transformation for moderately skewed data
# For positive skew
dat <- dat %>%
  mutate(variable_sqrt = sqrt(variable))

# For negative skew
dat <- dat %>%
  mutate(variable_sqrt = sqrt(max(variable + 1) - variable))
What this is doing:

For positive skew, sqrt(variable) takes the square root of every value. Since square roots compress large values more than small ones, this pulls in the long right tail characteristic of positive skew, bringing the distribution closer to symmetric.
For negative skew, the transformation is first reflected before taking the square root: max(variable + 1) - variable flips the distribution’s direction (turning a left-skewed tail into a right-skewed one), and adding 1 to the max ensures no value in the result becomes exactly 0, since a square root transformation can’t meaningfully handle 0 or negative values sitting right at the boundary. sqrt(...) is then applied to that reflected version.



10.6.5.2 Log transformation for highly skewed data
# For positive skew
dat <- dat %>%
  mutate(variable_log = log10(variable))

# For negative skew
dat <- dat %>%
  mutate(variable_log = log10(max(variable + 1) - variable))
What this is doing:

log10(variable) applies a base-10 logarithm to every value. Logs compress large values far more aggressively than a square root does, which is why this is the next step up when a square root transformation isn’t sufficient to resolve stronger skew.
Same reflection logic as above applies for negative skew: max(variable + 1) - variable flips the distribution’s direction before the log is applied, and the + 1 again avoids taking the log of 0 (which is mathematically undefined and would return -Inf).







WarningWarning



log10() (and log()) will return NaN for any negative value, and -Inf for exactly 0. If your variable can genuinely contain 0 or negative values (not just as an artifact of the reflection step above), you’ll need to shift the whole variable to be positive first — e.g. log10(variable + 1) — before transforming, or the function will silently produce unusable values for those rows.




10.6.5.3 Inverse transformation for severely skewed data
# For positive skew
dat <- dat %>%
  mutate(variable_inverse = 1 / variable)

# For negative skew
dat <- dat %>%
  mutate(variable_inverse = 1 / (max(variable + 1) - variable))
What this is doing:

1 / variable takes the reciprocal of every value — the strongest of the three transformations here, and the most aggressive at compressing extreme values. This is generally reserved for cases where square root and log transformations have both been tried and haven’t sufficiently resolved the skew.
Same reflection approach applies for negative skew, flipping the distribution’s direction with max(variable + 1) - variable before taking the reciprocal.







WarningWarning



I would recommend against the inverse transformation, to be honest. It reverses the order of your values, which makes the transformed variable much harder to interpret.




10.6.5.4 After transforming
Whichever transformation you use, re-run the skew/kurtosis check, Shapiro-Wilk test, and histogram/Q-Q plot on the new transformed variable to confirm whether the transformation actually resolved the normality issue.



10.6.6 Should you transform?
Some data is genuinely, meaningfully non-normal by nature. Social change and collective action data is a common example in this space (e.g., measures like willingness to protest, donation behavior, or petition-signing frequently show floor effects with a long right tail of highly engaged responders). This is often a real, substantive feature of the underlying phenomenon, not a data quality issue (Ferr, 2025).
Rather than defaulting straight to transformation, a few approaches are designed to handle non-normal data directly:

Non-parametric tests (e.g. Mann-Whitney U instead of an independent-samples t-test, Spearman’s rho instead of Pearson’s r) don’t assume normality in the first place, and handle skewed or ordinal data well.
Bootstrapping/resampling methods estimate variability empirically from your actual data, rather than relying on distributional assumptions — a flexible option when the shape of your data doesn’t map onto standard textbook methods.
Robust regression estimators (e.g. M-estimators, or the Satorra-Bentler scaled correction commonly used in structural equation modeling) can produce more accurate standard errors and significance tests under non-normality, without requiring you to transform the variable itself first.

title: 10  Data Cleaning: Step-by-Step
text:
10.7 Step 8: Multivariate outlier analysis
This identifies participants who are unusual in their pattern of responses and flags them. This coded uses all base r functions. Run this on the raw scores, not the Winsorised/transformed scores.
# Multivariate outlier detection
dat$mahal_d <- mahalanobis(
  dat[, c("var1", "var2", "var3", "var4")], 
  colMeans(dat[, c("var1", "var2", "var3", "var4")], na.rm=T), 
  cov(dat[, c("var1", "var2", "var3", "var4")], use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
)

dat$mahal_p <- pchisq(dat$mahal_d, df = numberofvariables, lower.tail = FALSE) #Note that you need to insert your number of variables included in the mahalanobis distance calculation above.

# Flag multivariate outliers
dat <- dat %>%
  mutate(mv_outlier = ifelse(mahal_p < 0.001, 1, 0))
What this is doing:

mahalanobis(...) calculates the Mahalanobis distance for every participant — a single number representing how far that participant’s overall pattern of responses across var1–var4 is from the “average” pattern, accounting for how those variables typically relate to each other. This is different from checking each variable separately: a participant could have entirely unremarkable individual scores, but still be a multivariate outlier if that particular combination of scores is unusual given how the variables normally correlate.
The function needs three pieces, matching its three arguments here:

dat[, c("var1", "var2", "var3", "var4")] — the set of variables you’re checking.
colMeans(..., na.rm = TRUE) — the average score for each variable, which acts as the “center” that every participant’s pattern is compared against.
cov(..., use = "pairwise.complete.obs") — the covariance matrix, capturing how the variables relate to and vary with each other. use = "pairwise.complete.obs" handles missing data.

The result, stored in dat$mahal_d, is one Mahalanobis distance value per participant — larger values mean a more unusual overall response pattern.
pchisq(dat$mahal_d, df = numberofvariables, lower.tail = FALSE) converts each distance into a p-value. lower.tail = FALSE gives you the probability of a distance at least this extreme occurring by chance — so a very small p-value means that participant’s response pattern would be highly unlikely to occur under normal, expected variation.
mutate(mv_outlier = ifelse(mahal_p < 0.001, 1, 0)) applies a conventional threshold — p < .001 is a commonly used cutoff for flagging multivariate outliers — and creates a simple 0/1 flag column, making it easy to filter, count, or review flagged cases afterward.

Typically, you would run your analysis on the full sample and then run sensitivity analyses excluding people with mv_outlier == 1 to see if the pattern of results holds without unusual participants.

title: 10  Data Cleaning: Step-by-Step
text:
10.8 Step 9: Missing value analysis
Next, run a missing value analysis to determine whether your data are missing completely at random. This uses the package naniar, and has a hold bunch of useful functions that are documented here.
For the most part, I only check the proportion of missing data on each variable, and use the Little’s MCAR test, unless I need to do some exploring.
First, run this code to determine the proportion of each variable which is missing data. Variables missing more than 30% are usually cause for concern.

library(naniar)
miss_var_summary(data)

# A tibble: 23 × 3
   variable   n_miss pct_miss
   <chr>       <int>    <num>
 1 trust_1        25        5
 2 trust_2        25        5
 3 identity_1     25        5
 4 identity_2     25        5
 5 identity_3     25        5
 6 efficacy_1     25        5
 7 efficacy_2     25        5
 8 efficacy_3     25        5
 9 efficacy_4     25        5
10 action_1       25        5
# ℹ 13 more rows


What this is doing:

This provides a summary for each variable of the number, percentage, and cumulative sum of missings, ordered by the most missing.

If there is no missing data on any of your key variables, there is no need to run Little’s MCAR test (Little, 1988). If there is missing data, run the following code. Note: I have included the individual items here, but you would normally run this test at the scale-level.

mcar_test(data[, c("trust_1", "trust_2", "identity_1", "identity_2", "identity_3", "efficacy_1", "efficacy_2", "efficacy_3", "efficacy_4", "action_1", "action_2", "action_3", "action_4", "action_5")])

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  statistic    df p.value missing.patterns
      <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>            <int>
1      905.   873   0.218               74


What this is doing:

Use Little’s (1988) test statistic to assess if data is missing completely at random (MCAR). The null hypothesis in this test is that the data is MCAR, and the test statistic is a chi-squared value.

Once you have cleaned your data, created your scales, you might want to generate sample demographics and correlations tables.
