title: 11  Data Quality
text:
11.1 Measures of response quality
There are lots of different ways to assess data quality, and different strategies for what to do with this information.

title: 11  Data Quality
text:
11.1.1 Instructed response attention checks
Instructed response attention checks have long been used in the world of assessing data quality. Questions are clear that they are attention checks (e.g., “To show you are paying attention, please select ‘Disagree’ in response to this question”). You might place several throughout the survey and decide on how many participants are able to fail before they are excluded from your final dataset. Alternatively, you may place two very early on in the survey and automatically screen out participants who fail both, while flagging those who failed one as potentially having lower quality responses.
My current strategy is the second option: participants respond to two attention checks in the first block of question; if they fail both, they are booted (with an explanation)—I think this is only appropriate to do if the checks are early on, so participants don’t feel like they have sacrificed too much of their time for a survey they will never be paid for.
I then flag participants who failed one attention check as having potentially lower quality responses (low_attention == 1 if 1/2 attention checks are failed, low_attention == 0 if both are passed), and use this as one indicator in a latent response quality variable assessed using item response theory (more on this at the bottom of the page).


11.1.2 Speeding
Participants who complete the study far too quickly to have meaningfully engaged with it are called speeders (Zhang & Conrad, 2014). Speeders often show other indicators of low response quality like straightlining, where they provide the same response repeatedly across a series of questions in order to complete the survey very quickly.

11.1.2.1 How to detect speeders
There are two common approaches for identifying participants who complete a survey too quickly.
First, you can decide in advance on a minimum completion time and exclude anyone who finishes faster than that as a blanket rule.
Second, you can determine what an unreasonably fast completion time is using the sample distribution. I typically calculate the survey’s average completion time and define speeding as a completion time that is more than three standard deviations below the mean. To ensure the average is representative, I exclude outliers (both high and low) from this estimate. Participants whose completion times fall more than three standard deviations below this adjusted are then flagged (my preference) or excluded.
Here is some example data, with some extreme low and extreme high outliers.

# Example usage
set.seed(123)
my_data <- c(rnorm(100, mean = 50, sd = 10), -250, -400, 560, 600)
mean(my_data)

[1] 53.85006


The below function finds the mean response time, excluding values +/- 3SD from the mean.

library(dplyr)


Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

# Function to calculate mean excluding outliers (+/- 3 SD)
mean_no_outliers <- function(x, sd_threshold = 3) {
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]  # remove NAs first
  m <- mean(x)
  s <- sd(x)
  
  lower <- m - sd_threshold * s
  upper <- m + sd_threshold * s
  
  x_trimmed <- x[x >= lower & x <= upper]
  
  return(mean(x_trimmed))
}

mean_no_outliers(my_data)

[1] 50.90406


If you want to know the outliers that were removed, you can run the following function. This code produces a tibble which will show you all the outlier values, and whether they are high or low outliers.

library(tibble)

identify_outliers <- function(x, sd_threshold = 3) {
  x_clean <- x[!is.na(x)]
  m <- mean(x_clean)
  s <- sd(x_clean)
  
  lower_bound <- m - sd_threshold * s
  upper_bound <- m + sd_threshold * s
  
  outlier_values <- x_clean[x_clean < lower_bound | x_clean > upper_bound]
  
  tibble(
    value = outlier_values,
    type = if_else(value < lower_bound, "low", "high")
  )
}

identify_outliers(my_data)

# A tibble: 4 × 2
  value type 
  <dbl> <chr>
1  -250 low  
2  -400 low  
3   560 high 
4   600 high 


Now that we know the adjusted mean, you can flag participants as speeders if their response time was more than 3SD below the adjusted mean. The below code flags participants as a speeder (1) if their duration was more than 3SD below the mean, and 0 if not.
data <- data %>%
  mutate(speeder = case_when(
    duration < (mean(duration) - 3*(sd(duration))) ~ 1,
    TRUE ~ 0
  ))
You can exclude based on this flag, see below, or include as an indicator to assess latent response quality (more on this later).
data <- data %>%
  filter(speeder == 0) # This will exclude participants that you have flagged as speeder



11.1.3 Straightlining
Straightlining is when participants have selected the same answer repeatedly across a series of questions, indicating they may not have provided meaningful answers. This measure tends to be associated with speeding (Zhang & Conrad, 2014).
Straightlining is most effective if you have both forward- and reverse-coded items for constructs, in which it would be extremely improbably for people to provide the same answers to all questions.

11.1.3.1 How to detect straightlining
The careless package contains many tools for assessing, as the name might imply, careless responding (Yentes & Wilhelm, 2023). One such measure is the longstring() function, which assesses straightlining. The longstring() function looks at a matrix of items and compares each response to the one before it, calculating the longest uninterrupted string of identical responses. That is, if a participants’ longstring score is 25, this means that they provided an identical response to 25 consecutive questions (and they have “straight-lined”). In this way, you only want to run the longstring() function on items that were measured on the same response scale.
library(dplyr)
library(careless)

data_careless <- data %>%
  dplyr::select(var1, var2, var3, var4, var5, var6, var7, var8, var9, var10) #Select the variables you want to check for straightlining

straightliner_results <- longstring(data_careless, avg = TRUE) 

avg = TRUE additionally returns the average length of uninterrupted identical responses for the participant.
This code will produce two variables in the “straightliner_results” dataframe called longstr and avgstr.

Then, append those new variables back into the main dataframe. The below code uses base R.
data$longstr <- straightliner_results$longstr
data$avgstr <- straightliner_results$avgstr
I don’t typically include many reverse-coded items in my surveys as they are known to have psychometric issues (Weijters & Baumgartner, 2012), and I usually include measures for many, many different variables. As such, it is completely possible that participants might show some level of straightlining in my surveys, and I don’t feel comfortable straight-up excluding participants based on this measure. However, I again use this as one indicator in a latent model of response quality. My approach is to find participants which show high outliers on this measure, and then flag them, using this flag as another indicator in a latent inattention variable.
The code below will tell the threshold above which you would flag people as being a high outlier on longstr and avgstr.
# Threshold for longstr
mean(data$longstr) + (3*sd(data$longstr))

# Threshold for avgstr 
mean(data$avgstr) + (3*sd(data$avgstr))

# Flag high outliers
data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    longstring_flag = case_when(longstr > (mean(longstr) + (3*sd(longstr))) ~ 1, TRUE ~ 0),
    avgstring_flag = case_when(avgstr > (mean(avgstr) + (3*sd(avgstr))) ~ 1, TRUE ~ 0)
  )



11.1.4 Item response variability
Item response variability (IRV) is a measure of within-person response variability and is often conceptually linked to straightlining. Respondents who provide low-quality data may be aware that researchers screen for straightlining and therefore attempt to avoid detection by responding in simple, repetitive patterns that nevertheless exhibit minimal variation. Consequently, low IRV scores, reflecting reduced within-person variability, may indicate patterned or non-differentiated responding (Dunn et al., 2018). Such cases can be identified by examining low-end outliers in the IRV distribution. At the same time, other researchers have argued that unusually high IRV scores may also signal problematic responding, as excessive variability can reflect random or careless response patterns (Marjanovic et al., 2015).

11.1.4.1 How to measure IRV
Again, the careless package (Yentes & Wilhelm, 2023) provides functions to assess IRV. As with straightlining, this assessment is only really useful on matrixes of continuously-scored items that share the same response scale.
The code below utilises the helper dataframe made in the previous section comprising relevant measures. A new variable called irv will be calculated, which is the standard deviation of responses across the set of items for each participant.
irv_values <- irv(data_careless, split = FALSE)
Append these values back into the main dataframe.
data$irv <- irv_values$irv
You can use the code from earlier to identify the high and low outliers.
identify_outliers <- function(x, sd_threshold = 3) {
  x_clean <- x[!is.na(x)]
  m <- mean(x_clean)
  s <- sd(x_clean)
  
  lower_bound <- m - sd_threshold * s
  upper_bound <- m + sd_threshold * s
  
  outlier_values <- x_clean[x_clean < lower_bound | x_clean > upper_bound]
  
  tibble(
    value = outlier_values,
    type = if_else(value < lower_bound, "low", "high")
  )
}

identify_outliers(data$irv)
You can then use the values to identify low and/or high thresholds beyond which participants will be flagged.
Alternatively, you can do this using the following code:
data <- data %>%
  mutate(irv_low = case_when(irv < (mean(irv) - (3*sd(irv))) ~ 1, TRUE ~ 0),
         irv_high = case_when(irv > (mean(irv) + (3*sd(irv))) ~ 1, TRUE ~ 0)
  )
Again, these flags can be used in a latent measure of response quality.



11.1.5 Seriousness check
Another measure I use is an explicit seriousness check presented as the final question in the survey, asking participants if they took part seriously or just clicked through. If they report that they just clicked through, their responses are flagged as being not serious (1), versus serious (0).


11.1.6 Latent response quality
Rather than excluding participants based on the above measures, I include them all as indicators of an underlying “response quality” measure using item response theory (Berinsky et al., 2013). Regardless of what measures of response quality are included in my survey design, I convert them all to a binary system of flags, as above, where 1 indicates a potentially low quality response as indicated by this measure and 0 suggests that the participant passes that particular check. These binary indicators are then modelled as indicators of an underlying latent “response quality” variable. This means that participants who score 1 on all of the response quality measures can be treated differently from participants who score 1 on only one or two. This latent variable can then be used to weight participant responses, as a covariate, or as another strategy to make exclusions for sensitivity analyses.
The strategy is as follows:
Prior to fitting a model, the unidimensionality of the response quality indicators is assessed using parallel analysis (Guo & Choi, 2023). This confirms whether a single latent factor adequately represents these measures.
library(mirt)
library(tidyr)

# Select the flag variables from the main dataset and ensure there are no NAs
flags <- data %>%
  dplyr::select(low_attention, speeder, longstr_flag, averagestr_flag, irv_low, irv_high, seriousness_check) %>%
  # Replace any remaining NAs with 0 (assuming non-flagged)
  mutate(across(everything(), ~replace_na(., 0)))

# Run parallel analysis
psych::fa.parallel(flags, cor = "tet", fa = "fa")

Parallel analysis checks how many real factors underlie the data by comparing the actual eigenvalues to eigenvalues generated from random, uncorrelated data of the same size. Any real eigenvalue that exceeds its random counterpart signals a genuine factor.
Parallel analysis is conducted using tetrachoric correlations rather than Pearson correlations for binary data, as here (Guo & Choi, 2023).
This code will tell you how many factors are underlying the indicators. 1 factor indicates that they all represent the same underlying response quality measure – if more than 1 factor emerges, it might be worth investigating this further to see which measures hang together and whether they represent different measures of response quality.

Next, these indicators are modelled as items in a unidimensional two-parameter logistic model (2PL) to estimate a latent response quality factor for careless responding using the mirt package (Chalmers, 2012). The 2PL model is the most appropriate choice for survey data because the measures will vary in how well they measure the underlying construct (response quality) and will vary in how difficult it is for participants to ‘trigger’ the flag, but there is no guessing involved (such as in tests where people can potentially guess the correct answer) which would justify the use of a three-parameter logic model (Edwards & Edelen, 2009).
# Fit a unidimensional 2PL model
irt_model <- mirt(data = flags, 
                  model = 1,        
                  itemtype = "2PL", 
                  verbose = FALSE)
What this is doing:

model = 1 tells mirt to fit a single-factor (unidimensional) model — appropriate if the parallel analysis confirmed one factor.



itemtype = "2PL" fits a two-parameter logistic model to each item. For each of the flags, this estimates two parameters:

Discrimination (a): how strongly that flag distinguishes between high- and low-response-quality participants. A flag with a high discrimination value is a “good” indicator: it clearly separates people who are genuinely careless from those who aren’t. A flag with low discrimination is a noisier or weaker signal of the underlying construct.
Difficulty/severity (b): how extreme someone’s underlying “low quality” trait needs to be before they trip that particular flag. Some flags (e.g., a strict speeder cutoff) might only get triggered by very low-quality responders, while others might get triggered more easily.




verbose = FALSE suppresses the iteration output during estimation

# Extract latent response quality scores and add to data
data$response_quality <- fscores(irt_model)[, 1]

fscores() estimates each participant’s position on the latent response-quality continuum, given their pattern of 0/1 flags and considering the discrimination/difficulty of each indicator. Higher values on this scale represent lower response quality (more evidence of careless responding), assuming 1 = fail on each of the indicators. This is the variable you can later use in sensitivity analyses.

# View the item parameters
print(coef(irt_model, simplify = TRUE))

This prints the a (discrimination) and b (difficulty) estimates for each of the flags.

# Optional: Check model fit
summary(irt_model)

This gives you factor loadings (a standardised version of the a parameters) and the proportion of variance in the flags explained by the single latent factor. This is the evidence that the 2PL solution is behaving sensibly (or not).

The resulting latent inattention scores can then be used in sensitivity analyses. For example, latent scores could be used as a control or weight in models, or a threshold can be set beyond which participants are flagged and sensitivity analyses are run with and without these participants.




Berinsky, A. J., Margolis, M. F., & Sances, M. W. (2013). Separating the shirkers from the workers? Making sure respondents pay attention on self-administered surveys. American Journal of Political Science, 58(3), 739–753. https://doi.org/10.1111/ajps.12081


Chalmers, R. P. (2012). mirt: A multidimensional item response theory package for the R environment. Journal of Statistical Software, 48(6), 1–29. https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v048.i06

title: 12  Overview
text:
You could run your analyses and then manually build your tables, copying over every relevant value by hand as you write up your results section. Or… you could use packages and functions to do that for you.
The benefit of this is threefold. First, it saves you time and cognitive effort, since you’re no longer trying to remember exactly which values a given psychology journal expects to see reported, in what order, and to how many decimal places. Second, it removes a huge number of opportunities for human error. Rather than manually copying dozens (or hundreds) of values — many of which may need updating repeatedly throughout drafting, revision, and re-analysis — you can handle this directly in R, so your tables update automatically whenever your underlying analysis changes. Finally, it allows your tables to be reproducible, so that if other researchers download your R code from your OSF repository, they, too, can create the same tables you have created.
In this section, I have included the code to create reproducible sample demographics tables and correlations tables. The code to produce tables for specific analyses can be found on the respective analysis pages.

title: 13  Sample Descriptives
text:
13.1 Using the tableone package
The first artifact in your manuscript will probably be a table of sample descriptives.
This example shows an end-to-end workflow: recoding numeric-coded demographic variables into labeled factors, building a “Table 1”-style summary table with tableone, converting it into a formatted flextable object, and exporting the result directly to a Word document.
The tableone package is great because it’s whole purpose is literally this, so no other functionality is needed. However, it does not allow exporting directly to a Word document, so I have provided a function to do this that some other fantastic, altruistic genius created and made freely available.
Packages needed:

title: 13  Sample Descriptives
text:
dplyr (or tidyverse) (Wickham et al., 2019)
tableone (Yoshida & Bartel, 2022)
flextable (Gohel & Skintzos, 2026)
officer (Gohel et al., 2026)


13.1.1 Step 1: Recode numeric codes into labelled factors
Now let’s recode.

data <- data %>%   
  mutate(gender_r = factor(gender,                           
                           levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4),                           
                           labels = c("Male", "Female", "Non-binary", "Other")),
         state_r = factor(state,
                          levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8),
                          labels = c("NSW", "VIC", "QLD", "SA", "WA", "TAS", "NT", "ACT")),
         education_r = factor(education,
                              levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5),
                              labels = c("Less than high school", "High school", "TAFE", "Undergraduate degree", "Postgraduate degree")))

What this is doing:

This builds on the factor-conversion approach, but adds a labels = argument alongside levels =. levels tells R the order and original numeric values to match against; labels tells it what text to display in place of each number — so 1 becomes "Male", 2 becomes "Female", and so on.



13.1.2 Step 2: Build the summary table with createTableOne()

library(tableone)  

vars <- c("age", "gender_r", "education_r")  # Select variables
table_1 <- CreateTableOne(data = data,
                          vars = vars,
                          strata = "state_r",
                          addOverall = TRUE,
                          test = FALSE)

What this is doing:

vars <- c("age", "gender_r", "education_r") defines which variables should appear as rows in the table..
CreateTableOne() builds the summary table itself. It automatically detects each variable’s type and picks an appropriate summary: means and SDs for continuous variables like age, counts and percentages for factors like gender_rand education_r— you don’t need to specify this manually.
strata = "state_r" splits the table into separate columns, one per state, so you can see how demographics differ by state at a glance, rather than only getting one pooled summary. This is useful if you have a representative survey; however, you could also list this as another count variable like gender and education. Similarly, you could add in an ethnicity variable, if relevant.
addOverall = TRUE adds an additional column showing the summary across all participants combined, alongside the state-by-state breakdown. This is useful if you have stratified the table, as we have done here.
test = FALSE turns off tableone’s default behavior of automatically running significance tests (e.g. ANOVA, chi-square) comparing groups and adding a p-value column. Turn this off if the table is meant to be purely descriptive (as Table 1s usually are). Turn this on with TRUE if you specifically want group comparisons included.
Printing table_1 on its own displays the table in your R console.



13.1.3 Step 3: Converting the tableone object into a flextable
This is a function helpfully created by Graham Cooper (Cooper, 2024) and made available on GitHub to quickly convert a tableone object to something that you can open in a Word document using the flextable and officer packages. Make sure you have those two packages installed and loaded to be able to run the function.

tableone2flextable <- function(tableone) {

  tableone <- print(
    tableone,
    quote = FALSE,
    noSpaces = TRUE
  )

  rows <- nrow(tableone)
  cols <- ncol(tableone)
  column_names <- colnames(tableone)

  listoflists <- vector("list", cols)

  for (i in seq_len(cols)) {

    start <- (i - 1) * rows + 1
    end <- i * rows

    listoflists[[i]] <- tableone[start:end]
  }

  dataframe <- as.data.frame(
    listoflists,
    col.names = column_names
  )

  dataframe <- cbind(
    Variable = rownames(tableone),
    dataframe
  )

  rownames(dataframe) <- NULL

  flextable::flextable(dataframe)
}

What this is doing:

print(tableone, quote = FALSE, noSpaces = TRUE) converts the tableone object into a plain character matrix, stripping out quote marks and extra padding spaces that tableone normally adds for console display.
nrow(), ncol(), and colnames() capture the matrix’s dimensions and column headers, which are needed to correctly rebuild the table as a dataframe in the next steps.
The for loop walks through the matrix column by column (1:cols), extracting each column’s values from the matrix (which is stored internally as one long vector) using start/end index positions, and stores each column as one element of listoflists.
as.data.frame(listoflists, col.names = colnames) combines those extracted columns into an actual dataframe, restoring the original column headers.
cbind(Variable = rownames(tableone), dataframe) adds the row labels (variable names like “age,” “gender_r”) back in as their own proper column, since they’d otherwise just be dataframe row names rather than visible data.
rownames(dataframe) <- NULL resets the dataframe’s row names back to plain sequential numbers, since the row names were just used above and aren’t needed anymore as actual row labels.
flextable::flextable(dataframe) converts the finished dataframe into a flextable object — the format needed for the polished formatting and Word export in the next step. Using flextable:: here (rather than library(flextable) beforehand) explicitly specifies which package the function comes from, without needing to load the whole package into your session.



13.1.4 Step 4: Format and export to Word

flextable2word <- function(flextab, filepath) {

  ft <- flextable::theme_apa(flextab) %>%
    flextable::autofit() %>%
    flextable::align(
      j = 1,
      align = "left",
      part = "all"
    ) %>%
    flextable::line_spacing(
      space = 1,
      part = "all"
    )

  officer::read_docx() %>%
    flextable::body_add_flextable(ft) %>%
    officer::body_end_section_landscape() %>%
    print(target = filepath)

  browseURL(filepath)
}

Save and open Word document.
What this is doing:

flextable2word is a second custom function that takes a flextable object (from Step 3) and a filepath argument, letting you specify exactly where and under what filename the finished Word document should be saved.
require(tidyverse) and require(flextable) ensure both packages are loaded whenever this function runs, even if you forgot to load them earlier in your script.
theme_apa(flextab) applies flextable’s built-in APA-style formatting preset.
autofit(...) automatically adjusts column widths to fit their content, rather than leaving default widths that might cut off longer text.
align(j = 1, align = "left", part = "all") left-aligns the first column (j = 1, i.e. your Variable column) across the whole table (part = "all" covers header and body).
line_spacing(space = 1, part = "all") sets single line spacing throughout the table, overriding Word’s common default of double spacing, which looks cluttered in a table context.
officer::read_docx() starts a new, blank Word document object using the officer package (which flextable relies on for Word export) — officer:: here again explicitly specifies the package without a separate library() call.
body_add_flextable(ft) inserts your formatted table into that blank document.
body_end_section_landscape() sets the page orientation to landscape for this section — useful for wider tables (like this one, with multiple state columns) that wouldn’t fit well on a standard portrait page.
print(target = filepath) writes the finished Word document out to disk, at the location and filename you specify in filepath.
browseURL(filepath) automatically opens the newly created Word file once it’s saved, so you can immediately check the result without manually navigating to find it.



13.1.5 Step 5: Run it
flextable2word(
  tab_1,
  "output/Table 1 Sample demographics.docx"
)
What this is doing:

tableone2flextable(table_1) runs the Step 3 function on the table you built in Step 2, producing the flextable version.
flextable2word(tab_1, "output/Table 1 Sample demographics.docx") runs the Step 4 function, exporting the finished, formatted table straight to a Word file in your output/ folder.
Running tab_1 on its own at the end displays the flextable directly in your R/RStudio viewer, letting you do a final visual check.

title: 14  Table of Correlations
text:
The second table you will probably create in your manuscript is the table of intercorrelations. I use the apaTables package to do this.

apaTables (Stanley, 2026)


library(apaTables)
corr_table <- apa.cor.table(data[, c("trust", "identity", "efficacy", "action")], 
                        table.number = 2, 
                        show.sig.stars = TRUE, 
                        landscape = TRUE,
                        filename = "output/Table 2 Correlations pooled sample.docx")

What this is doing:

corr_table <- saves the created table to an object in your R environment.
data[, c("var1", "var2", "var3", "var4")] selects the variables to include, otherwise it will produce a correlations table for every variable in the dataframe.
table.number = 2 allows you to set the number of the table in the produced artifact.
landscape = TRUE is pretty self-explanatory, it creates the table in a landscape oriented Word document. This might not be necessary if you only have a few variables; in this case, set this to FALSE.
filename = "output/Table 2 Correlations pooled sample.doc" saves the table to a word document in your ‘outputs’ folder in your R project.

You can then call it in your R environment using the code corr_table.

title: 15  T-Tests
text:
15.1 Running a single t-test
I use the rempsyc package to run t-tests, which is a “wrapper” on top of other functions designed to produce APA-formatted t-test tables directly. Rather than running a t-test, then manually formatting the output into a table, rempsyc does both in two function calls.
Packages required:
Let’s create some fake data to demonstrate.
set.seed(1234)

dat <- data.frame(
  grouping_variable = rep(c("Control", "Treatment"), each = 50),

  dependent_variable = c(
    rnorm(50, mean = 4.0, sd = 1.0),
    rnorm(50, mean = 4.8, sd = 1.1)
  ),

  var1 = c(
    rnorm(50, 4.0, 1.0),
    rnorm(50, 4.7, 1.0)
  ),

  var2 = c(
    rnorm(50, 5.2, 1.2),
    rnorm(50, 5.8, 1.1)
  ),

  var3 = c(
    rnorm(50, 3.8, 0.9),
    rnorm(50, 4.0, 0.9)
  ),

  var4 = c(
    rnorm(50, 4.5, 1.0),
    rnorm(50, 5.4, 1.1)
  )
)
And to run the t-test and create the results table using rempsyc…
library(rempsyc)

Suggested APA citation: Thériault, R. (2023). rempsyc: Convenience functions for psychology. 
Journal of Open Source Software, 8(87), 5466. https://doi.org/10.21105/joss.05466

t_test_results <- nice_t_test(
  data = dat,
  response = "dependent_variable",
  group = "grouping_variable"
)

Using independent samples t-test. 
 


Using Welch t-test (base R's default; cf. https://doi.org/10.5334/irsp.82).
For the Student t-test, use `var.equal = TRUE`. 
 

t_test_table <- nice_table(t_test_results)
t_test_table

Dependent Variabletdfpd95% CIdependent_variable-6.8992.29< .001***-1.38[-1.81, -0.94]
What this is doing:

title: 15  T-Tests
text:
nice_t_test() runs the actual t-test. Unlike base R’s t.test(), it returns results already formatted for reporting, including the test statistic, degrees of freedom, p-value, and an effect size (Cohen’s d), all in one output.
response = "dependent_variable" and group = "grouping_variable" are given as character strings (in quotes) rather than bare column names.
nice_table() takes the results from nice_t_test() and converts them into a polished, APA-styled table, with leading zeros dropped.

title: 15  T-Tests
text:
15.2 Running multiple t-tests at once
The function accepts either a single variable or a vector of several variables (e.g. response = c("var1", "var2", "var3")) to run multiple t-tests at once, all formatted consistently in the same output.

t_test_results <- nice_t_test(
  data = dat,
  response = c("var1", "var2", "var3", "var4"),
  group = "grouping_variable",
  correction = "bonferroni"
)

Using independent samples t-test. 
 


Using Welch t-test (base R's default; cf. https://doi.org/10.5334/irsp.82).
For the Student t-test, use `var.equal = TRUE`. 
 

nice_table(t_test_results)

Dependent Variabletdfpd95% CIvar1-3.5889.41.002**-0.72[-1.12, -0.31]var2-2.0596.32.172-0.41[-0.81, -0.01]var3-0.9189.081.461-0.18[-0.57, 0.21]var4-4.6187.70< .001***-0.92[-1.33, -0.51]


What this is doing:

correction = "bonferroni" applies a Bonferroni correction to the p-values across the four tests.
Bonferroni is, of course, the most conservative correction for multiple tests. You can select the Holm correction (correction = "holm") or false discovery rate methods (correction = "fdr") for less conservative corrections that are also supported by nice_t_test().

Perneger (1998) discusses when Bonferroni adjustments might be the wrong choice, and Benjamini & Hochberg (1995) explain the false discovery rate method.

title: 15  T-Tests
text:
15.3 Running t-tests using lavaan/SEM frameworks
I can’t imagine this being particularly useful to anyone but me, but I once had to figure out how to fit a t-test but using full information maximum likelihood (FIML). For background, a large representative survey contained around 50% missingness by design (to reduce questionnaire completion time and cognitive load for participants). Running standard t-tests would therefore require lisewise deletion of a devastatingly large amount of data.
My supervisor asked me to devise a way to run t-tests using FIML estimation, which allows partially missing cases to be retained and contribute to the overall model estimation, reducing bias.1 However, FIML is an estimation method used in structural equation modelling and is not something that has been widely incorporated into other statistical analyses. As such, I had to figure out how to run the equivalent of a t-test using the SEM framework.
In order to run a t-test equivalent with FIML in R, the following packages are required:

lavaan [Rosseel et al. (2026)](Rosseel, 2012)


15.3.1 Building the models
Essentially what I wanted to do was compare two different models: one in which the mean of the variable of interest is constrained to be equal (equivalent to the null hypothesis) and one in which the mean is able to vary (alternative hypothesis). I used the multigroups function in lavaan, which allowed me to specify the grouping variable. Variance in the dependent variables is freely estimated across groups to approximate a Welch t-test when group sizes are unequal. The models are then compared. A significant likelihood ratio test indicates that the unconstrained model fit the data better than the constrained model; therefore, the groups differed significantly.
The code below shows a full workflow in which I listed all the variables I wanted to run t-tests on (in this case, many!), created an analysis function, looped it over all the dependent variables, and exported the results to a table with correction applied for multiple testing.


15.3.2 Step 1: List dependent variables
`%||%` <- function(a, b) if (!is.null(a)) a else b

dv_config_controlled <- list(
  list(dv = "stress",                  scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "dangerous",               scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "emotional_wb",            scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "psych_wb",                scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "swb_contribution",        scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "swb_acceptance",          scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "swb_integration",         scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "swb_own_actualisation",   scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "swb_other_actualisation", scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "swb_coherence",           scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "anomie",                  scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "prej_immigrant_s",        scale_factor = 10),
  list(dv = "prej_muslim_s",           scale_factor = 10),
  list(dv = "prej_jewish_s",           scale_factor = 10),
  list(dv = "collangst",               scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "fear_immigrants",         scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "norms_immigration_s",     scale_factor = 10),
  list(dv = "norms_racism_s",          scale_factor = 10),
  list(dv = "norms_demo_s",            scale_factor = 10),
  list(dv = "poleff",                  scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "sysjust",                 scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "demo_indecisive",         scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "demo_best_option",        scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "undemo",                  scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "polviol",                 scale_factor = NULL),
  list(dv = "anger_govt",              scale_factor = NULL)
)
What this is doing:

%||% <- function(a, b) if (!is.null(a)) a else b defines a custom operator, %||%, borrowed from a common pattern in other languages. It returns a if a isn’t NULL, and falls back to b otherwise. It’s used later as a shorthand: dv_config_controlled[[i]]$scale_factor %||% 1 means “use this DV’s specified scale_factor, or default to 1 if none was given” — this is exactly what lets you leave scale_factor = NULL for any DV that doesn’t need rescaling, without writing a separate if/else check for every single variable.
dv_config_controlled is a list of DVs to run the models over (equivalent to running multiple t-tests). In this particular case, most of my variables ranged from 1-7 but a select few were on 0-100 scales. Estimating a model with variables on wildly different numeric scales can cause convergence problems in SEM software, since parameters for the larger-scale variables end up many times bigger than parameters for the smaller-scale ones. Dividing the 0–100 variables by 10 brings them onto a comparable 0–10 range, closer to the other DVs, which helps the model estimate more reliably.
If every variable is already on a comparable scale, you can simply set scale_factor = NULL for every entry (or omit it entirely and let %||% default to 1 throughout).



15.3.3 Step 2: Created scaled variables
# ── Scale prejudice and norms variables from 0-100 to 0-10 ────────────────────
group_comparisons <- group_comparisons %>%
  mutate(
    prej_jewish_s        = prej_jewish / 10,
    prej_immigrant_s     = prej_immigrant / 10,
    prej_muslim_s        = prej_muslim / 10,
    norms_immigration_s  = norms_immigration / 10,
    norms_racism_s       = norms_racism / 10,
    norms_demo_s         = norms_demo / 10)
What this is doing:

Created the rescaled variables referred to in dv_config_controlled



15.3.4 Step 3: Define auxiliary variables
COMMON_AUXILIARY <- "gender_r"
What this is doing:

FIML estimates model parameters using all available data, including partially missing cases.
An auxiliary variable is a variable that isn’t part of your substantive hypothesis (it’s not a DV or a theoretically important predictor), but is included in the model purely to improve the plausibility of that assumption and increase estimation precision. You want to include a variable that has very little missing data, thereby giving FIML more information to work with when handling the missing cases.

This code is written for one common auxiliary variable, applied to every DV. You can adapt it either direction:

For zero auxiliary variables: just set COMMON_AUXILIARY <- NULL. The use_auxiliary check inside the function (below) already handles this — if common_auxiliary is NULL, the auxiliary syntax is simply left blank, and the model runs without one.
For multiple auxiliary variables: you’d adjust the aux_syntax construction to loop over a vector of auxiliary variables instead of a single one:

COMMON_AUXILIARIES <- c("gender_r", "AGE", "EDUC")

if (length(common_auxiliaries) > 0) {
  aux_lines <- unlist(lapply(common_auxiliaries, function(aux) {
    paste0(
      "\n  ", aux, " ~ 1",
      "\n  ", dv_name, " ~~ ", aux
    )
  }))
  aux_syntax <- paste(aux_lines, collapse = "\n")
} else {
  aux_syntax <- ""
}


15.3.5 Step 4: Mean-centring covariates
If you wanted to add controls/covariates to the model, you must mean-centre continuous variables and ensure that dichotomous variables are effect-coded.
group_comparisons <- group_comparisons %>%
  mutate(
    AGE_c  = AGE  - mean(AGE,  na.rm = TRUE),
    EDUC_c = EDUC - mean(EDUC, na.rm = TRUE),
    CONS_c = CONS - mean(CONS, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

group_comparisons <- group_comparisons %>%
  mutate(VIC = case_when(
    STATE_2 == 0 ~ -1,
    TRUE ~ 1
  ))
What this is doing:

AGE_c, EDUC_c, and CONS_c are mean-centered versions of the covariates (subtracting each variable’s own mean from every value), which makes the model’s intercept interpretable as the predicted value at the average level of these covariates, rather than at a covariate value of zero (which is often meaningless — e.g. an age of 0).
VIC is created using effect coding (covered earlier in this chapter) rather than standard 0/1 dummy coding, so this covariate’s coefficient reflects deviation from the grand mean rather than from an arbitrary reference group.



15.3.6 Step 5: The analysis function
run_group_comparison_controlled <- function(dv_name, common_auxiliary = NULL, data,
                                            scale_factor = 1) {
  
  # Build auxiliary syntax — a single common auxiliary variable used for every DV
  # (skip it if the DV itself happens to be the common auxiliary)
  use_auxiliary <- !is.null(common_auxiliary) && common_auxiliary != dv_name
  
  if (use_auxiliary) {
    aux_syntax <- paste0(
      "\n  # Auxiliary variable - estimate its mean\n  ",
      common_auxiliary, " ~ 1",
      "\n\n  # Covariance between DV and auxiliary\n  ",
      dv_name, " ~~ ", common_auxiliary
    )
  } else {
    aux_syntax <- ""
  }
  
  # Unconstrained model
  model_unconstrained <- paste0("
  ", dv_name, " ~ c(mu1, mu2)*1
  ", dv_name, " ~ AGE_c + EDUC_c + CONS_c + VIC
  ", dv_name, " ~~ c(v1, v2)*", dv_name, aux_syntax, "

  diff     := mu2 - mu1
  sd_welch := sqrt((v1 + v2) / 2)
  d_welch  := diff / sd_welch
")
  
  # Constrained model (means equal across groups)
  model_constrained <- paste0("
  ", dv_name, " ~ c(mu_equal, mu_equal)*1
  ", dv_name, " ~ AGE_c + EDUC_c + CONS_c + VIC
  ", dv_name, " ~~ c(v1, v2)*", dv_name, aux_syntax, "
")
  
  fit_unconstrained <- sem(
    model_unconstrained,
    data          = data,
    group         = "pre_post",
    meanstructure = TRUE,
    fixed.x       = FALSE,
    missing       = "FIML"
  )
  
  fit_constrained <- sem(
    model_constrained,
    data          = data,
    group         = "pre_post",
    meanstructure = TRUE,
    fixed.x       = FALSE,
    missing       = "FIML"
  )
  
  # Verify group ordering (pre = 1, post = 2)
  group_labels <- lavInspect(fit_unconstrained, "group.label")
  if (!identical(group_labels, c("pre", "post"))) {
    stop(
      "Unexpected group ordering for '", dv_name, "': ",
      paste(group_labels, collapse = ", "),
      ". Expected c('pre', 'post')."
    )
  }
  
  # Sample sizes
  N_total <- lavInspect(fit_unconstrained, "nobs")
  n1      <- N_total[1]
  n2      <- N_total[2]
  
  # Likelihood ratio test
  lr_test <- anova(fit_constrained, fit_unconstrained)
  chi_sq  <- lr_test$`Chisq diff`[2]
  df      <- lr_test$`Df diff`[2]
  p_raw   <- lr_test$`Pr(>Chisq)`[2]
  
  # Extract parameters
  params    <- parameterEstimates(fit_unconstrained, ci = TRUE)
  means     <- subset(params, op == "~1" & lhs == dv_name)
  diff_row  <- subset(params, label == "diff")
  d_row     <- subset(params, label == "d_welch")
  variances <- subset(params, op == "~~" & lhs == dv_name & rhs == dv_name)
  
  # Back-transform scale-dependent outputs; Cohen's d is dimensionless so no transform
  data.frame(
    variable = sub("_s$", "", dv_name),   # strip _s suffix for display
    
    pre_M   = round(means$est[means$group == 1]         * scale_factor, 2),
    pre_var = round(variances$est[variances$group == 1] * scale_factor^2, 2),
    
    post_M   = round(means$est[means$group == 2]         * scale_factor, 2),
    post_var = round(variances$est[variances$group == 2] * scale_factor^2, 2),
    
    diff_est = round(diff_row$est      * scale_factor, 2),
    diff_lci = round(diff_row$ci.lower * scale_factor, 2),
    diff_uci = round(diff_row$ci.upper * scale_factor, 2),
    
    d_est = round(d_row$est,      2),
    d_lci = round(d_row$ci.lower, 2),
    d_uci = round(d_row$ci.upper, 2),
    
    chi_sq  = round(chi_sq, 2),
    df      = df,
    p_raw   = p_raw,
    
    n_pre   = n1,
    n_post  = n2,
    n_total = sum(N_total),
    
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}
What this is doing:

This is a custom function, written once, then looped over every DV in the next step.
use_auxiliary <- !is.null(common_auxiliary) && common_auxiliary != dv_name checks two things: that an auxiliary variable was actually specified, and that it isn’t the same variable as the DV currently being tested (which would be nonsensical). If both are true, aux_syntax is built (the auxiliary’s mean is estimated, and its covariance with the DV specified) — this is the lavaan syntax that actually implements the FIML auxiliary-variable approach.
model_unconstrained is the alternative hypothesis model: c(mu1, mu2)*1 estimates a separate mean for each of the two groups (labeled mu1 and mu2) — this is the “the groups genuinely differ” model.

~~ c(v1, v2)*dv_name estimates a separate variance per group too, which is what makes this approximate a Welch t-test (which doesn’t assume equal variances) rather than a standard Student’s t-test.
The diff, sd_welch, and d_welch lines are lavaan’s := syntax for defining new quantities derived from the model’s parameters — here, calculating the mean difference and a Welch-style Cohen’s d directly as part of the model, rather than needing a separate calculation step afterward.

model_constrained is your null hypothesis model: c(mu_equal, mu_equal)*1 forces both groups to share the same mean — this is “the groups don’t actually differ” model, everything else kept identical.
sem(..., missing = "FIML") is what actually runs each model using full information maximum likelihood — this single argument is what allows participants with partial missing data to still contribute to parameter estimation, rather than being dropped via listwise deletion, addressing the exact problem described in your intro paragraph.
group = "pre_post" tells lavaan which variable defines your two comparison groups — the model is fit separately per group, which is what allows the means (and variances) to differ between them in the unconstrained model.
The group_labels check is a safeguard: lavaan orders groups alphabetically/by factor level internally, and if that order ever didn’t match your expectation ("pre" first, "post" second), any downstream code assuming group == 1 is “pre” would silently mislabel results. stop() halts execution with a clear error message the moment this happens, rather than letting a silent labeling mistake flow through to your final table.
lavInspect(..., "nobs") extracts the sample size used in each group.
anova(fit_constrained, fit_unconstrained) runs a likelihood ratio test, comparing how well the two models fit the data. This is the “t-test equivalent”: rather than a t-statistic, you get a chi-square difference test, where a significant result means the unconstrained model (means allowed to differ) fits meaningfully better than the constrained one (means forced equal) — i.e. the groups differ significantly.
The params block tpulls every result out of the fitted model — group means, variances, the mean difference with its confidence interval, and Cohen’s d with its confidence interval — and assembles them into a single tidy row of output for this one DV.

This is where scale_factor does its work: pre_M * scale_factor, post_M * scale_factor, and the difference/CI values are all multiplied back up, undoing the /10 rescaling, so the variables are reported back on their original, interpretable 0–100 scale.
d_est, d_lci, d_uci are not multiplied by scale_factor — Cohen’s d is a standardised effect size (already expressed in SD units), so it’s scale-invariant by design and doesn’t need back-transforming.
sub("_s$", "", dv_name) also strips the _s suffix back off the variable name for the final table, so it reads as "prej_immigrant" rather than the working name "prej_immigrant_s".




15.3.7 Step 6: Running every comparison in a loop
results_list_controlled <- vector("list", length(dv_config_controlled))
for (i in seq_along(dv_config_controlled)) {
  cat("\n#### Comparison", i, "of", length(dv_config_controlled), ":",
      dv_config_controlled[[i]]$dv, "####\n")
  results_list_controlled[[i]] <- run_group_comparison_controlled(
    dv_name           = dv_config_controlled[[i]]$dv,
    common_auxiliary  = COMMON_AUXILIARY,
    scale_factor      = dv_config_controlled[[i]]$scale_factor %||% 1,
    data              = group_comparisons
  )
}

final_results_controlled <- do.call(rbind, results_list_controlled)
rownames(final_results_controlled) <- NULL
What this is doing:

vector("list", length(...)) pre-allocates an empty list of the right size — more efficient than growing a list one element at a time in a loop.
The for loop then runs run_group_comparison_controlled() once per DV listed in dv_config_controlled, printing a progress message (cat(...)) each time so you can track how far through a long list of comparisons you are.
The scale_factor = dv_config_controlled[[i]]$scale_factor %||% 1 line is exactly where the Step 2 operator gets used in practice — pulling each DV’s own specified scale_factor, or defaulting to 1 if it was left NULL.
do.call(rbind, results_list_controlled) stacks every DV’s one-row result into a single combined dataframe, and rownames(...) <- NULL tidies up the row numbering.



15.3.8 Step 7: Correcting for multiple comparisons
final_results_controlled$p_adj_BH         <- p.adjust(final_results_controlled$p_raw, method = "BH")
final_results_controlled$p_adj_bonferroni <- p.adjust(final_results_controlled$p_raw, method = "bonferroni")
What this is doing:

p.adjust() is base R’s general-purpose function for correcting a whole vector of p-values at once.
Two different methods have been applied: Bonferroni and BH (which is the false discovery rate method).



15.3.9 Step 8: Formatting and exporting the table
format_results_table_controlled <- function(results) {
  format_mean_sd <- function(mean, var) sprintf("%.2f (%.2f)", mean, sqrt(var))
  format_mean_ci <- function(mean, lower, upper) sprintf("%.2f [%.2f, %.2f]", mean, lower, upper)
  
  data.frame(
    Variable          = results$variable,
    `Pre M (SD)`      = mapply(format_mean_sd, results$pre_M,  results$pre_var),
    `Post M (SD)`     = mapply(format_mean_sd, results$post_M, results$post_var),
    `Mean difference` = sprintf("%.2f", results$diff_est),
    `Cohen's d [95% CI]` = mapply(format_mean_ci, results$d_est, results$d_lci, results$d_uci),
    `Chi-square`      = sprintf("%.2f", results$chi_sq),
    p                 = results$p_raw,
    `p (FDR)`         = results$p_adj_BH,
    `p (Bonf)`        = results$p_adj_bonferroni,
    check.names = FALSE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}
What this is doing:

This function turns the raw numeric results into publication-ready formatted text — e.g. combining a mean and its variance into a single "3.45 (1.02)" style string (converting variance to SD first, via sqrt(var), since SD rather than variance is the conventional format for reporting), and combining Cohen’s d with its confidence interval into "0.32 [0.18, 0.46]" style APA formatting.
check.names = FALSE stops R from automatically mangling your deliberately spaced/punctuated column names (like "Pre M (SD)") into syntactically “safe” but ugly names (like "Pre.M..SD.").

nice_formatted_table_controlled <- nice_table(
  formatted_table_controlled,
  col.format.p = 7:9,
  spacing      = 1,
  title        = c("Table S3", "Pre-post comparisons controlling for age, education, and conservatism, with raw, BH-corrected, and Bonferroni-corrected p-values.")
)

sect_properties <- prop_section(
  page_size    = page_size(orient = "landscape"),
  page_margins = page_mar(top = 0.5, bottom = 0.5, left = 0.5, right = 0.5),
  type         = "continuous"
)

save_as_docx(
  nice_formatted_table_controlled,
  path       = "Tables/Table 3 Pre-post comparisons AGE EDUC CONS STATE_2 controlled.docx",
  pr_section = sect_properties
)
What this is doing:

Using nice_table() from the rempsyc package, APA styling has been applied to the finished dataframe.
col.format.p = 7:9 tells it specifically which columns (7 through 9 — the raw, FDR, and Bonferroni p-value columns) should be formatted using APA’s p-value conventions (e.g. p < .001 rather than a long decimal).
title adds a table number and caption directly into the exported table.
prop_section() (from the officer package) defines a landscape page layout with tight margins, needed here because this table is unusually wide (nine columns).
save_as_docx() is rempsyc’s own direct Word export function.





Benjamini, Y., & Hochberg, Y. (1995). Controlling the false discovery rate: A practical and powerful approach to multiple testing. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B (Methodological), 57(1), 289–300. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2517-6161.1995.tb02031.x

Perneger, T. V. (1998). What’s wrong with bonferroni adjustments. BMJ, 316(7139), 1236–1238. https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.316.7139.1236

title: 18  Structural Equation Modelling
text:
18.1 Covariance-based SEM
The code below is taken from Pittaway et al. (2024). This was a complicated model that included two observed IVs, three observed DVs, and a parallel mediation model comprising one observed and one latent variable. In order to calculate the specific indirect and total effects, you must specify names for each indirect effect and assign them an alphabetical code.
Note: I am pasting this code here so that others can access it as needed, but I will return to tidy up this page and annotate the code more comprehensively when I have time. If you are trying to run a similar model and it’s throwing errors, let me know and I can help troubleshoot.

title: 18  Structural Equation Modelling
text:
The theoretical model being tested in Pittaway et al. (2024).




18.1.1 Specify the model
library(lavaan)
library(semTools)

### M1 ###
m1 <- '
# LATENT VARIABLES
ANX =~ anx_aff + anx_behav + anx_rumin + anx_pers

# STRUCTURAL MODEL (Direct Effects)
conv_private ~ e*ANX + a*ecas + l*future + o*immediate
conv_public ~ g*ANX + c*ecas + m*future + p*immediate
rad_public ~ h*ANX + d*ecas + n*future + q*immediate
ecas ~ b*future + j*immediate
ANX ~ f*future + k*immediate + i*ecas

# COVARIANCES
conv_private ~~ conv_public + rad_public
conv_public ~~ rad_public

# INDIRECT AND TOTAL EFFECTS

## CFC -> PRIV
#Indirect effect 1: cfc -> aeca -> priv
#Indirect effect 2: cfc -> aeca -> anx -> priv
#Indirect effect 3: cfc -> anx -> priv

de_cfc_1 := l
ie_cfc_1 := b*a
ie_cfc_2 := b*i*e
ie_cfc_3 := f*e
TI_cfc_1 := ie_cfc_1 + ie_cfc_2 + ie_cfc_3
TE_cfc_1 := l + TI_cfc_1


## CFC -> PUB
#Indirect effect 4: cfc -> aeca -> pub
#Indirect effect 5: cfc -> aeca -> anx -> pub
#Indirect effect 6: cfc -> anx -> pub

de_cfc_2 := m
ie_cfc_4 := b*c
ie_cfc_5 := b*i*g
ie_cfc_6 := f*g
TI_cfc_2 := ie_cfc_4 + ie_cfc_5 + ie_cfc_6
TE_cfc_2 := m + TI_cfc_2


## CFC -> RAD
#Indirect effect 7: cfc -> aeca -> rad
#Indirect effect 8: cfc -> aeca -> anx -> rad
#Indirect effect 9: cfc -> anx -> rad

de_cfc_3 := n
ie_cfc_7 := b*d
ie_cfc_8 := b*i*h
ie_cfc_9 := f*h
TI_cfc_3 := ie_cfc_7 + ie_cfc_8 + ie_cfc_9
TE_cfc_3 := n + TI_cfc_3



## CIC -> PRIV
#Indirect effect 1: cic -> aeca -> priv
#Indirect effect 2: cic -> aeca -> anx -> priv
#Indirect effect 3: cic -> anx -> priv

de_cic_1 := o
ie_cic_1 := j*a
ie_cic_2 := j*i*e
ie_cic_3 := k*e
TI_cic_1 := ie_cic_1 + ie_cic_2 + ie_cic_3
TE_cic_1 := o + TI_cic_1


## CIC -> PUB
#Indirect effect 4: cic -> aeca -> pub
#Indirect effect 5: cic -> aeca -> anx -> pub
#Indirect effect 6: cic -> anx -> pub

de_cic_2 := p
ie_cic_4 := j*c
ie_cic_5 := j*i*g
ie_cic_6 := k*g
TI_cic_2 := ie_cic_4 + ie_cic_5 + ie_cic_6
TE_cic_2 := p + TI_cic_2


## CIC -> RAD
#Indirect effect 7: cic -> aeca -> rad
#Indirect effect 8: cic -> aeca -> anx -> rad
#Indirect effect 9: cic -> anx -> rad

de_cic_3 := q
ie_cic_7 := j*d
ie_cic_8 := j*i*h
ie_cic_9 := k*h
TI_cic_3 := ie_cic_7 + ie_cic_8 + ie_cic_9
TE_cic_3 := q + TI_cic_3



# AECA -> PRIV
#Indirect effect 1: aeca -> anx -> priv

de_aeca_1 := a
ie_aeca_1 := i*e
TE_aeca_1 := a + ie_aeca_1

# AECA -> PUB
#Indirect effect 2: aeca -> anx -> pub

de_aeca_2 := c
ie_aeca_2 := i*g
TE_aeca_2 := c + ie_aeca_2

# AECA -> RAD
#Indirect effect 3: aeca -> anx -> rad

de_aeca_3 := d
ie_aeca_3 := i*h
TE_aeca_3 := d + ie_aeca_3


# CFC -> ANX
#Indirect effect 1: cfc -> aeca -> anx

de_anx_1 := f
ie_anx_1 := b*i
TE_anx_1 := f + ie_anx_1

# CIC -> ANX
#Indirect effect 2: cic -> aeca -> anx

de_anx_2 := k
ie_anx_2 := j*i
TE_anx_2 := k + ie_anx_2
'


18.1.2 Run the model
# Run model
fit_m1 <- sem(m1, dat_s1, missing = "fiml", se = "bootstrap")
fitmeasures(fit_m1, fit.measures = c("chisq", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "rmsea", "srmr"))

# Get standardized path coefficients
summary(fit_m1, standardized = T) 

# Check modification indices > 3.85
modificationindices(fit_m1, sort. = T, minimum.value = 3.85) 
What this is doing:

Full information maximum likelihood estimation is turned on, which means that partially missing cases are not dropped from the model and are instead used to improve the estimation of model effects.
se = "bootstrap" turns on bootstrapped standard errors instead of the model’s default (usually asymptotic, formula-based) standard errors. Bootstrapping works by repeatedly resampling your data (with replacement) many times, refitting the model on each resample, and using the spread of results across those resamples to estimate how much your parameter estimates would vary from sample to sample.

This is a common choice when your data doesn’t meet the normality assumptions that standard SEM standard errors rely on. It makes fewer distributional assumptions, at the cost of being more computationally intensive.

fitmeasures(fit_m1, fit.measures = c(...)) extracts specific model fit statistics, rather than printing every fit measure lavaan can calculate (which is a long list by default).

cfi and tli (Comparative Fit Index and Tucker-Lewis Index). Both compare the model to a baseline “null” model, with values closer to 1 indicating better fit, and values above .95 conventionally considered good (Kline, 2015).
rmsea (Root Mean Square Error of Approximation). This is a measure of how well the model approximates the true population covariance structure, with values below .06 conventionally considered good fit (Kline, 2015).
srmr (Standardized Root Mean Square Residual). This is the average discrepancy between observed and model-implied correlations, with values below .08 conventionally considered acceptable (Hu & Bentler, 1999).
Note that the chi-square statistic and corresponding p-value is very sensitive to sample size (Kline, 2015).

summary(fit_m1, standardized = TRUE) prints the full model summary, including all path coefficients, variances, and covariances — standardized = TRUE adds an extra column showing standardized coefficients, which are the ones typically reported in a results write-up.
modificationindices(fit_m1, sort. = TRUE, minimum.value = 3.85) checks for potential missing paths in your model — for every parameter not currently included, it estimates how much your model’s chi-square would improve if that parameter were added.

sort. = TRUE orders the output from largest to smallest improvement, so the most impactful potential additions appear first.
minimum.value = 3.85 filters out trivial suggestions, only showing modification indices above this threshold — 3.85 corresponds to roughly p < .05 on a chi-square distribution with 1 degree of freedom, so this is filtering to changes that would represent a statistically meaningful improvement in fit if added.




18.1.3 Multigroup SEM
To meaningfully compare across groups, measurement invariance must first be established Kline (2015). A baseline model is established by estimating the parameters separately for each group, and configural invariance is met if the fit is acceptable and if the pattern of factor loadings is consistent. The baseline model is then compared to increasingly more constrained models. First, the factor loadings are constrained to be equal, and metric invariance is met if doing so does not worsen model fit compared to baseline. Then, intercepts are constrained in addition to the factor loadings, and scalar invariance is met if this more constrained model does not decrease model fit. For all measurement invariance models, the chi-square is reported; however, the practical fit indices and change in CFI and RMSEA (ΔCFI < .01 and ΔRMSEA < .015 for large samples; Chen (2007)) were used to assess changes to model fit.

18.1.3.1 Build the multigroup model
If assessing indirect effects, the model will have to be built slightly differently so that there are unique parameters for each indirect/total effect for each group. The following code is again taken from Pittaway et al. (2024).
### M2 - Separate Defined Parameters ###
m2 <- '
# LATENT VARIABLES
ANX =~ anx_aff + anx_behav + anx_rumin + anx_pers

# STRUCTURAL MODEL (Direct Effects)
conv_private ~ c(e1, e2)*ANX + c(a1, a2)*ecas + c(l1, l2)*future + c(o1, o2)*immediate
conv_public ~ c(g1, g2)*ANX + c(c1, c2)*ecas + c(m1, m2)*future + c(p1, p2)*immediate
rad_public ~ c(h1, h2)*ANX + c(d1, d2)*ecas + c(n1, n2)*future + c(q1, q2)*immediate
ecas ~ c(b1, b2)*future + c(j1, j2)*immediate
ANX ~ c(f1, f2)*future + c(k1, k2)*immediate + c(i1, i2)*ecas

# COVARIANCES
conv_private ~~ conv_public + rad_public
conv_public ~~ rad_public

# INDIRECT AND TOTAL EFFECTS

# GROUP 1

## CFC -> PRIV
#Indirect effect 1: cfc -> aeca -> priv
#Indirect effect 2: cfc -> aeca -> anx -> priv
#Indirect effect 3: cfc -> anx -> priv

de_cfc_1_1 := l1
ie_cfc_1_1 := b1*a1
ie_cfc_2_1 := b1*i1*e1
ie_cfc_3_1 := f1*e1
TI_cfc_1_1 := ie_cfc_1_1 + ie_cfc_2_1 + ie_cfc_3_1
TE_cfc_1_1 := l1 + TI_cfc_1_1


## CFC -> PUB
#Indirect effect 4: cfc -> aeca -> pub
#Indirect effect 5: cfc -> aeca -> anx -> pub
#Indirect effect 6: cfc -> anx -> pub

de_cfc_2_1 := m1
ie_cfc_4_1 := b1*c1
ie_cfc_5_1 := b1*i1*g1
ie_cfc_6_1 := f1*g1
TI_cfc_2_1 := ie_cfc_4_1 + ie_cfc_5_1 + ie_cfc_6_1
TE_cfc_2_1 := m1 + TI_cfc_2_1


## CFC -> RAD
#Indirect effect 7: cfc -> aeca -> rad
#Indirect effect 8: cfc -> aeca -> anx -> rad
#Indirect effect 9: cfc -> anx -> rad

de_cfc_3_1 := n1
ie_cfc_7_1 := b1*d1
ie_cfc_8_1 := b1*i1*h1
ie_cfc_9_1 := f1*h1
TI_cfc_3_1 := ie_cfc_7_1 + ie_cfc_8_1 + ie_cfc_9_1
TE_cfc_3_1 := n1 + TI_cfc_3_1



## CIC -> PRIV
#Indirect effect 1: cic -> aeca -> priv
#Indirect effect 2: cic -> aeca -> anx -> priv
#Indirect effect 3: cic -> anx -> priv

de_cic_1_1 := o1
ie_cic_1_1 := j1*a1
ie_cic_2_1 := j1*i1*e1
ie_cic_3_1 := k1*e1
TI_cic_1_1 := ie_cic_1_1 + ie_cic_2_1 + ie_cic_3_1
TE_cic_1_1 := o1 + TI_cic_1_1


## CIC -> PUB
#Indirect effect 4: cic -> aeca -> pub
#Indirect effect 5: cic -> aeca -> anx -> pub
#Indirect effect 6: cic -> anx -> pub

de_cic_2_1 := p1
ie_cic_4_1 := j1*c1
ie_cic_5_1 := j1*i1*g1
ie_cic_6_1 := k1*g1
TI_cic_2_1 := ie_cic_4_1 + ie_cic_5_1 + ie_cic_6_1
TE_cic_2_1 := p1 + TI_cic_2_1


## CIC -> RAD
#Indirect effect 7: cic -> aeca -> rad
#Indirect effect 8: cic -> aeca -> anx -> rad
#Indirect effect 9: cic -> anx -> rad

de_cic_3_1 := q1
ie_cic_7_1 := j1*d1
ie_cic_8_1 := j1*i1*h1
ie_cic_9_1 := k1*h1
TI_cic_3_1 := ie_cic_7_1 + ie_cic_8_1 + ie_cic_9_1
TE_cic_3_1 := q1 + TI_cic_3_1



# AECA -> PRIV
#Indirect effect 1: aeca -> anx -> priv

de_aeca_1_1 := a1
ie_aeca_1_1 := i1*e1
TE_aeca_1_1 := a1 + ie_aeca_1_1

# AECA -> PUB
#Indirect effect 2: aeca -> anx -> pub

de_aeca_2_1 := c1
ie_aeca_2_1 := i1*g1
TE_aeca_2_1 := c1 + ie_aeca_2_1

# AECA -> RAD
#Indirect effect 3: aeca -> anx -> rad

de_aeca_3_1 := d1
ie_aeca_3_1 := i1*h1
TE_aeca_3_1 := d1 + ie_aeca_3_1


# CFC -> ANX
#Indirect effect 1: cfc -> aeca -> anx

de_anx_1_1 := f1
ie_anx_1_1 := b1*i1
TE_anx_1_1 := f1 + ie_anx_1_1

# CIC -> ANX
#Indirect effect 2: cic -> aeca -> anx

de_anx_2_1 := k1
ie_anx_2_1 := j1*i1
TE_anx_2_1 := k1 + ie_anx_2_1




# GROUP 2

## CFC -> PRIV
#Indirect effect 1: cfc -> aeca -> priv
#Indirect effect 2: cfc -> aeca -> anx -> priv
#Indirect effect 3: cfc -> anx -> priv

de_cfc_1_2 := l2
ie_cfc_1_2 := b2*a2
ie_cfc_2_2 := b2*i2*e2
ie_cfc_3_2 := f2*e2
TI_cfc_1_2 := ie_cfc_1_2 + ie_cfc_2_2 + ie_cfc_3_2
TE_cfc_1_2 := l2 + TI_cfc_1_2


## CFC -> PUB
#Indirect effect 4: cfc -> aeca -> pub
#Indirect effect 5: cfc -> aeca -> anx -> pub
#Indirect effect 6: cfc -> anx -> pub

de_cfc_2_2 := m2
ie_cfc_4_2 := b2*c2
ie_cfc_5_2 := b2*i2*g2
ie_cfc_6_2 := f2*g2
TI_cfc_2_2 := ie_cfc_4_2 + ie_cfc_5_2 + ie_cfc_6_2
TE_cfc_2_2 := m2 + TI_cfc_2_2


## CFC -> RAD
#Indirect effect 7: cfc -> aeca -> rad
#Indirect effect 8: cfc -> aeca -> anx -> rad
#Indirect effect 9: cfc -> anx -> rad

de_cfc_3_2 := n2
ie_cfc_7_2 := b2*d2
ie_cfc_8_2 := b2*i2*h2
ie_cfc_9_2 := f2*h2
TI_cfc_3_2 := ie_cfc_7_2 + ie_cfc_8_2 + ie_cfc_9_2
TE_cfc_3_2 := n2 + TI_cfc_3_2



## CIC -> PRIV
#Indirect effect 1: cic -> aeca -> priv
#Indirect effect 2: cic -> aeca -> anx -> priv
#Indirect effect 3: cic -> anx -> priv

de_cic_1_2 := o2
ie_cic_1_2 := j2*a2
ie_cic_2_2 := j2*i2*e2
ie_cic_3_2 := k2*e2
TI_cic_1_2 := ie_cic_1_2 + ie_cic_2_2 + ie_cic_3_2
TE_cic_1_2 := o2 + TI_cic_1_2


## CIC -> PUB
#Indirect effect 4: cic -> aeca -> pub
#Indirect effect 5: cic -> aeca -> anx -> pub
#Indirect effect 6: cic -> anx -> pub

de_cic_2_2 := p2
ie_cic_4_2 := j2*c2
ie_cic_5_2 := j2*i2*g2
ie_cic_6_2 := k2*g2
TI_cic_2_2 := ie_cic_4_2 + ie_cic_5_2 + ie_cic_6_2
TE_cic_2_2 := p2 + TI_cic_2_2


## CIC -> RAD
#Indirect effect 7: cic -> aeca -> rad
#Indirect effect 8: cic -> aeca -> anx -> rad
#Indirect effect 9: cic -> anx -> rad

de_cic_3_2 := q2
ie_cic_7_2 := j2*d2
ie_cic_8_2 := j2*i2*h2
ie_cic_9_2 := k2*h2
TI_cic_3_2 := ie_cic_7_2 + ie_cic_8_2 + ie_cic_9_2
TE_cic_3_2 := q2 + TI_cic_3_2



# AECA -> PRIV
#Indirect effect 1: aeca -> anx -> priv

de_aeca_1_2 := a2
ie_aeca_1_2 := i2*e2
TE_aeca_1_2 := a2 + ie_aeca_1_2

# AECA -> PUB
#Indirect effect 2: aeca -> anx -> pub

de_aeca_2_2 := c2
ie_aeca_2_2 := i2*g2
TE_aeca_2_2 := c2 + ie_aeca_2_2

# AECA -> RAD
#Indirect effect 3: aeca -> anx -> rad

de_aeca_3_2 := d2
ie_aeca_3_2 := i2*h2
TE_aeca_3_2 := d2 + ie_aeca_3_2


# CFC -> ANX
#Indirect effect 1: cfc -> aeca -> anx

de_anx_1_2 := f2
ie_anx_1_2 := b2*i2
TE_anx_1_2 := f2 + ie_anx_1_2

# CIC -> ANX
#Indirect effect 2: cic -> aeca -> anx

de_anx_2_2 := k2
ie_anx_2_2 := j2*i2
TE_anx_2_2 := k2 + ie_anx_2_2
'


18.1.3.2 Measurement invariance
First, establish the baseline model by estimating the parameters separately for each group.
# Establish baseline model by estimating parameters separately for each group
baseline_1 <- sem(m2, dat_s1, group = "counterbalancing", missing = "fiml") 

fitmeasures(baseline_1, fit.measures = c("chisq", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "rmsea", "srmr"))

summary(baseline_1)

Configural invariance is met if the model fits and the pattern of factor loadings is the same for each group.

Next, establish metric invariance by constraining the factor loadings to be equal.
# Establish metric invariance by constraining the factor loadings to be equal
metric_1 <- sem(m2, dat_s1, group = "counterbalancing", group.equal = "loadings", missing = "fiml", se = "bootstrap")

fitmeasures(metric_1, fit.measures = c("chisq", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "rmsea", "srmr"))
Next, establish scalar invariance by constraining factor loadings + observed means to be equal.
# Establish scalar invariance by constraining factor loadings + observed means to be equal
scalar_1 <- sem(m2, dat_s1, group = "counterbalancing", group.equal = c("loadings", "intercepts"), se = "bootstrap", missing = "fiml")

fitmeasures(scalar_1, fit.measures = c("chisq", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "rmsea", "srmr")) 

# Compare models
summary(compareFit(baseline_1, metric_1, scalar_1))

Metric invariance is established if the change in CFI and RMSEA are below the recommended thresholds.
Scalar invariance is established if the change in CFI and RMSEA are below the recommended thresholds.

If scalar invariance is established, then the model is appropriately measurement-invariant across groups which means you can meaningfully compare the pathways and effect sizes. At this point, you can get the standardised coefficients for the scalar model. If you were creating a new scale, however, and you would feasibly expect that the groups SHOULD be the same, you would have to establish one final level of invariance (the strictest level ever), which I cannot currently remember. If it becomes relevant to me, I’ll update this page.






NoteNote



If running complicated models with bootstrapping, expect the models to take a long time to estimate. This is normal!




18.1.3.3 Get multigroup model coefficients
# Get standardised coefficients
summary(scalar_1, standardized = T)

title: 18  Structural Equation Modelling
text:
18.2 Partial-least-squares SEM
Regular SEM, as outlined above, is covariance-based SEM (CB-SEM). CB-SEM is primarily used to confirm or reject theories and their underlying hypotheses. It works by determining how closely a proposed theoretical model can reproduce the covariance matrix for an observed sample dataset.
Partial least squares SEM (PLS-SEM) is a second method of conducting SEM. It was introduced more as a ‘causal-predictive’ approach to SEM, and focuses on explaining the variance in the model’s DVs. It’s also better at coping with smaller samples and complex models. Another good thing about PLS-SEM is that it is non-parametric, meaning that non-normally distributed data is not a problem.
It uses a different approach to analysis that is explained in detail in the following sources:

Hair, J. F., Ringle, C. M., & Sarstedt, M. (2011). PLS-SEM: Indeed a silver bullet. Journal of Marketing Theory and Practice, 19(2), 139–152. https://doi.org/10.2753/MTP1069-6679190202
Hair, J. F., Hult, G. T. M., Ringle, C. M., Sarstedt, M., Danks, N. P., & Ray, S. (2021). Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) Using R: A Workbook. Springer International Publishing. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-80519-7
Hair, J. F., Hult, G. T. M., Ringle, C. M., Sarstedt, M., Danks, N. P., & Ray, S. (2021). Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) Using R: A Workbook. Springer International Publishing. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-80519-7

The one downside is that there are no established goodness-of-fit indicators, so its use for theory testing and confirmation is pretty limited. But, if you have already run confirmatory analyses showing support for your model (e.g., in an earlier paper), then PLS-SEM can overcome sample size/distributional issues.
Packages required:

cSEM (https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/cSEM/vignettes/cSEM.html)
lavaan (https://lavaan.ugent.be/tutorial/)

The cSEM package uses lavaan syntax to run the PLS-SEM.

18.2.1 Build the model using lavaan
The following code is taken from Pittaway et al. (2025). This tests practically the same model as in the example above, except with additional longer indirect pathways to the three DVs.



The theoretical model being tested in Pittaway et al. (2025.


### M1 ###
m1_csem <- '
ANX <~ anx_aff_w1 + anx_behav_w1 + anx_rumin_w1 + anx_pers_w1
CFC <~ cfc_w1
CIC <~ cic_w1
PRIV <~ conv_priv_w1
PUB <~ conv_pub_w1
RAD <~ rad_pub_w1
ECAS <~ ecas_w1
aPRIV <~ act_conv_priv_w2
aPUB <~ act_conv_pub_w2
aRAD <~ act_rad_pub_w2

# STRUCTURAL MODEL 
PRIV ~ ANX + ECAS + CFC + CIC
PUB ~ ANX + ECAS + CFC + CIC
RAD ~ ANX + ECAS + CFC + CIC
ECAS ~ CFC + CIC
ANX ~ CFC + CIC + ECAS
aPRIV ~ PRIV
aPUB ~ PUB
aRAD ~ RAD
'
Note: Unlike regular SEM, the cSEM package cannot handle the separate indirect effects.


18.2.2 Implement PLS-SEM
# Basic implementation
results_csem <- csem(
  .data = dat, 
  .model = m1_csem, 
  .approach_weights = "PLS-PM",
  .approach_paths = "OLS"
  )

bootstrap_results <- resamplecSEMResults(
  .object = results_csem,
  .resample_method = "bootstrap",
  .R = 1000,
  .seed = 123456
)

# Assess measurement model
results_csem

# View results
summarize(bootstrap_results)
results_csem$Estimates$R2
verify(results_csem)

assess(bootstrap_results)
predict(bootstrap_results, .approach_predict = "direct")


18.2.3 Evaluate + interpret the model
First, assess the variance inflation factors (VIFs) to ensure they are all below the recommended threshold of 3, which indicates that collinearity that would otherwise bias the regression results is not a problem in the present data (Hair et al., 2019).
Evaluation of the structural model is done in the following ways:

Significance of the hypothesised paths
The size of the effects
The explanatory power of the model (R2): R2 values greater than .75, .50, and .25 denoted substantial, moderate, and weak power, respectively (Hair et al., 2011).
The predictive relevance of the model (Q2): Q2 values of greater than 0, .25, and .50 indicated small, medium, and large predictive relevance, while negative values indicated no predictive relevance (Hair et al., 2011).



18.2.4 Manually calculate indirect effects
The total indirect effects and total effects are generated in the initial output, but you have to write a function to generate the specific indirect pathways if you have multiple. This code was adapted from https://github.com/FloSchuberth/cSEM/issues/457.
A separate function is created for every unique indirect pathway in the model. This is an example for one unique indirect pathway.
# Define function to calculate the specific indirect effect CFC -> PRIV -> aPRIV
indirectCFC_PRIV_aPRIV <- function(.object) {
  .object$Estimates$Path_estimates['PRIV', 'CFC'] * .object$Estimates$Path_estimates['aPRIV', 'PRIV']
}

# Run cSEM with the user-defined function
result_indirectCFC_PRIV_aPRIV <- csem(
  .data = dat,
  .model = m1_csem,
  .resample_method = "bootstrap",
  .R = 100,
  .seed = 147150973,
  .user_funs = indirectCFC_PRIV_aPRIV
)

# Get the coefficient of the indirect effect
result_indirectCFC_PRIV_aPRIV$Estimates$Estimates_resample$Estimates1
infer(result_indirectCFC_PRIV_aPRIV, .quantity = 'CI_percentile')




Chen, F. F. (2007). Sensitivity of goodness of fit indices to lack of measurement invariance. Structural Equation Modeling: A Multidisciplinary Journal, 14(3), 464–504. https://doi.org/10.1080/10705510701301834


Hair, J. F., Risher, J. J., Sarstedt, M., & Ringle, C. M. (2019). When to use and how to report the results of PLS-SEM. European Business Review, 31(1), 2–24. https://doi.org/10.1108/EBR-11-2018-0203


Hu, L., & Bentler, P. M. (1999). Cutoff criteria for fit indexes in covariance structure analysis: Conventional criteria versus new alternatives. Structural Equation Modeling: A Multidisciplinary Journal, 6(1), 1–55. https://doi.org/10.1080/10705519909540118


Kline, R. B. (2015). Principles and practice of structural equation modeling. Guilford Publications.


Pittaway, C. R., Fielding, K. S., & Louis, W. R. (2024). Pathways to conventional and radical climate action: The role of temporal orientation, environmental cognitive alternatives, and eco-anxiety. Global Environmental Change, 87, 102886. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2024.102886


Pittaway, C. R., Louis, W., & Fielding, K. (2025). Temporal orientations and climate action over time: A two-wave study of the associations between consideration of consequences and climate action intentions and behaviour. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 108, 102833. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvp.2025.102833

title: 20  Themes and Plot Colouring
text:
20.1 APA-formatted figures
There are two approaches here: using a purpose-built theme from an existing package, or building the APA look manually with theme(). Both are worth knowing.
Packages required:

title: 20  Themes and Plot Colouring
text:
ggplot2 (part of tidyverse)
jtools (Long, 2024)


20.1.1 Using the theme_apa() function in ggplot2
#| eval: false
ggplot(data, aes(x = condition, y = outcome)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Condition", y = "Outcome Score") +
  theme_apa()
What this is doing:

theme_apa() applies a pre-built theme designed to match APA figure conventions in one line — removing gridlines, using a clean black-and-white base, and adjusting font/line styling to match APA’s expectations for published figures.







TipTip



theme_apa() has a few optional arguments worth knowing: legend.pos = "none" removes the legend entirely (useful if your plot doesn’t need one), and facet.title.size adjusts facet label sizing if you’re using facet_wrap()/facet_grid(). Run ?theme_apa for the full list, and visit this cookbook for the full ggplot2 customisation: (Chang, 2026).




20.1.2 Building the APA look manually
These are the APA conventions specified manually in ggplot2:
#| eval: false
ggplot(dat, aes(x = condition, y = outcome)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Condition", y = "Outcome Score") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    axis.line = element_line(colour = "black"),
    axis.text = element_text(colour = "black"),
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  )






TipTip



You can apply these APA formatting conventions to any kind of ggplot2 figure: just add the theme() part to the end of the plot code.

⭐A perfect angel of a package for reporting correlation tables, regression tables, and ANOVA tables with APA 7 specifications.
The downside of this package is that it has a very narrow focus on just APA conventions and just these few tables types, with limited customisation/table building options.

Building a demographics table with Table One



gtsummary (Sjoberg et al., 2021)
https://www.danieldsjoberg.com/gtsummary/index.html
A more comprehensive, highly customisable package for creating publication-ready tables. ⭐Can present multiple regression models side-by-side in the one table (which apaTables cannot do). But I don’t know how to use it, so if you figure it out, let me know.



flextable (Gohel & Skintzos, 2026)
https://ardata-fr.github.io/flextable-book/index.html
Provides a framework to easily create tables for | reporting and publications. | | ⚠️It can be a bit difficult to use for a beginner, but I often embed it into my workflow to create custom tables (with a lot of help from the internet). | | | | | | | | | |


rempsyc (Thériault, 2023)
https://rempsyc.remi-theriault.com/index.html
This convenience package includes functions to easily create “nice” APA tables (i.e., correctly formatted). For example, lots of outputs created in R will save p-values, lower 95% CIs, and upper 95% CIs in separate columns. If these are named correctly, the nice_table() function in rempsych will automatically detect them and format them correctly. Similarly, you can easily create APA-formatted t-test tables, planned contrasts, moderation tables, violin plots, and scatter plots.
